card_text: April 27, 2022 Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health’s Study on the Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic Summary: The COVID-19 pandemic has had a dire effect on wait times for medical imaging procedures across the country. Canadians who may have postponed diagnostic and necessary follow-up imaging early in the pandemic are now in more urgent need of care. This has created a massive influx of patients attempting to reschedule their appointments, not to mention the additional treatment that will be required for those undiagnosed or with worsening conditions. If we do not take action, the increased pressure on radiology will catch up after the pandemic, the growing baseline demand for imaging, and the existing capacity limitations in radiology departments will combine to continue to damage the healthcare system. Excessive and lengthening wait times for medical imaging is reducing efficiency across our healthcare system and leading to burnout in our health workforce. Canadians are being diagnosed later into the progression of their ailments, leading to a reduction in treatment options, increased recovery time, and worsening patient outcomes. Investments must be made quickly to support more up-to-date modern imaging equipment and information technologies. Overview: Medical imaging is our healthcare system’s gateway for diagnosing diseases, and patients from every demographic rely on it to detect and treat these conditions. card_text: [ORG]’S STATEMENT FOR THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Monday April 4, 2022 Recommendation: That the government identify a plan for research funding to ‘invest in the study of the long-term health impacts of COVID-19, including of long COVID on different groups, notably vulnerable populations and children’ as noted within government mandate letters. Background What is long COVID? As we recover from the acute impacts of COVID-19, a new impact has begun to gain attention. Most people who develop COVID-19 resume a normal life about two weeks after their illness1. However some individuals experience long-term symptoms including cognitive issues, such as brain fog, headaches, dizziness, anxiety, and/or persistent respiratory symptoms1. These symptoms have been grouped together and called ‘long COVID’, or post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC), however the long-term impacts on lung health and support for management and/or treatment for those suffering largely remains unclear 2. Most common long lasting symptoms include shortness of breath, fatigue, and cognitive challenges2. These symptoms are not simply minor inconveniences. Many people experiencing long COVID describe them as ‘crushing’; ‘overwhelming’ ‘unmanageable’ and ‘unpredictable’ 2. Many of these patients may be experiencing symptoms that are challenging to explain. In fact a small pilot, which has since been expanded to a larger study, based out of the UK tested a new approach of using xenon gas to measure lung functioning3. In this pilot study, xenon gas mimicked oxygen in the lungs and demonstrated lung abnormalities amongst those suffering with long-lasting breathlessness from COVID. This was amongst individuals

card_text: [ORG] Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic February 2022 Recommendations 1. Increase specific funding for cataract surgeries and increased access to surgical operating room time. 2. Fund and provide increased access to mental health supports, specifically for physicians and allied health workers. 3. Create a Federal Vision Health Desk within Health Canada or PHAC to support the creation of a national vision strategy. Key Issues Impact of Covid-19 on surgery backlogs Jurisdictions worldwide ramped down ophthalmic surgeries to mitigate the effects of COVID-19, creating a global surgical backlog. Since the start of the pandemic in 2020, the overall estimated number of patients awaiting surgery grew by 129% and 150%, respectively. The estimated mean wait time for patients for all subspecialty surgeries in Ontario increased to 282 days in March 2021 compared with 94 days in 2019. The provincial monthly additional resources required to clear the backlog by March 2023 was estimated to be a 34% escalation from the pre-pandemic volumes (4626 additional surgeries). Cataract surgeries, one of the most common surgeries performed in Canada, has been particularly impacted. We know that cataract surgery is enormously important to patients: not only does this sightrestoring surgery increase their quality of life, but it also helps prevent falls and other injuries. The Conference Board of Canada report Ophthalmology in Canada has highlighted the importance of ophthalmological procedures and the associated costs savings to the health care system. For example, treating vision loss

card_text: Brief Prepared for: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Topic: National Medical Licensure; Petition e-3378 Author: Dr. Kyle Sue, MD, MHM, BSc, GCPain, CCFP(PC) Author Affiliations: a) Rural/Remote General Practitioner, Clinical Assistant Professor, Discipline of Family Medicine, Memorial University of Newfoundland b) Pediatric Palliative Care and Developmental Pediatrics Specialist, Clinical Assistant Professor, Department of Pediatrics, University of Alberta c) Assistant Professor, School of Rehabilitation Sciences, McMaster University Recommendation: Enact federal legislation to facilitate national mobility of medical professionals throughout all 13 provinces and territories of Canada, starting with at least the mobility of fully Canadian-certified physicians in good standing. Background: Canada’s healthcare system is in crisis. The COVID pandemic has removed the veil over our healthcare system’s pre-existing gaps and weaknesses. As we rode the waves of the pandemic, hard-hit jurisdictions, such as Greater Toronto, have required 'rescue teams' of healthcare professionals to travel from other provinces to relieve the sick and burned-out local medical providers.(1) This was not as easy as it seemed on the surface, however. The Government of Ontario had to have the foresight to enact the Emergency Management and Civil Protection Act Regulation 305/21.(2) It allowed fully licensed professionals from other provinces to begin work immediately in Ontario without going through the usual lengthy and expensive bureaucratic quagmire of licensure in another province. Without this, the thinly stretched system would have collapsed. Under the Canada Health Act, healthcare is under provincial jurisdiction. card_text: BRIEF BRIEFING ON THE EMERGENCY SITUATION FACING CANADIANS IN LIGHT OF THE COVID‐19 PANDEMIC Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health June 2021 Summary The COVID‐19 pandemic has exposed and exacerbated gaps in Canada’s health care system. Rural, remote, and Indigenous communities have faced unique challenges that must be addressed effectively. In regions hardest hit by outbreaks, health care workers have often found themselves short‐staffed, exhausted, and barely able to cope with the number of patients requiring care. These crises were often even more dire for vulnerable populations, particularly in rural and Indigenous communities. The mobilization of health care resources and health care providers from one part of the country to another to help communities in need was limited. This inability to recruit adequate numbers of health care professionals to meet increased needs has led many health care workers to suffer from burnout or illness. In many cases, the lack of a licence to practise in the jurisdiction was a key barrier to providing the help needed in rural, remote, and Indigenous communities. Just as we have seen these challenges laid bare, we have also been able to spotlight opportunities for improvement. The Society of Rural Physicians of Canada (SRPC) calls for a national approach to licensure that would allow health care professionals to work anywhere in Canada. This would enhance access to care for patients and increase the workforce coverage available to support and sustain health care professionals. As part of the House of Commons Standing

(I’m not saying Quebec mistreats the most vulnerable, but it does let everybody pick on them and their illnesses…) We should be protecting these people from everybody else and from our own institutions. We should be putting them safely into centres where they will feel comfortable and be taken care of for once, instead of leaving the mentally ill out in the streets, open to exploitation by all and sundry. Where have our social services gone? What are our social services doing? If social services won’t look after these people, who will? (No one.) It’s not their responsibility. The social workers aren’t responsible. Responsible for psychologically integrating our citizens. Quebec is gambling with people’s mental health, and that’s a dangerous game to play. card_text: Brief on the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis [ORG] ([ORG]) A Plea for Balance and Nuance [ORG] ([ORG]) is a Canadian charity founded in 2016 by parents of teenagers who developed substance use disorder (SUD) in their teens. [ORG] was founded because the needs of our families are not being met. We provide free peer support services across Canada to family members of those struggling with SUD. [ORG] provides one-on-one and group support, as well as a live phone support line. All of our volunteers are trained in the Invitation to Change Approach, which is a combination of Community Reinforcement and Family Training (CRAFT), Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) and Motivational Interviewing (MI). It is a non-confrontational, compassionate and evidence-based approach. We also educate about SUD and advocate for protective health laws and protective drug policies. [ORG] participated in the work of the Standards Council of Canada to develop the Mental Health and Substance Use Health Standardization Roadmap 1, as well as the work of Toronto Public Health to develop a decriminalization model for Toronto. As such, [ORG] may be uniquely positioned to make recommendations as we support decriminalization and modernizing our mental health acts, both of which are necessary if we are to treat substance use and SUD as a health issue and protect the safety of all. As has already been expressed by the variety of briefs and witnesses, this is a complex and tragic public health emergency with multiple viewpoints and considerations. It is also a topic of great urgency to Canadians, with over 47,000 lives lost to opioid-related toxicity since 20161. [ORG] is eager to support a submission from the national perspective of addiction medicine providers. Evidence-based policy requires expert and specialist points of view along with the added insight of the etiology and epidemiology of those at risk of, or living with, substance use disorders (SUDs). SUDs are best understood as chronic medical conditions, with multiple biopsychosocial factors underlying their development, often in adolescence or early adulthood, not through a moral failing or a criminal justice lens. A previous estimate of lifetime prevalence of a SUD2 in Canada is shockingly high, at 21%, or over 1 in 5 Canadians. Additionally, according to CIHI3, SUDs were the 5th most common cause of hospitalization in 2021-2022, reflecting a substantial impact on an already taxed medical system. card_text: Over the Influence Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis As the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis take deeper roots in Canada, youth have become a largely affected population with little to no support. According to the Government of Canada (2024), nearly 20% of total apparent opioid toxicity deaths were youth aged 0-to-29 years old. The impact on young people in Canada is profound and multifaceted, as the crisis impacts their health, wellbeing, and prospects. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), youth who engage in substance misuse are more likely to experience physical and sexual dating violence and are more susceptible to mental health and suicide risk (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2020). This epidemic has far-reaching effects on the youth demographic and is disrupting the lives and futures of our future leaders. As opioid-related poisonings and deaths escalate, there has been an uptake in interest around interventions. However, even though Canadians are being introduced to opioids and addiction at an earlier age, most interventions are based around the adult (25+) experience. According to Corace et al. (2018), a majority of the related research is in reference to adults, even though youth were 3-4 times more likely than adults 25 and older to report harm related to substance use in their physical health, social and work/study, legal, and housing problems. These interventions cannot adopt a one-size-fits-all approach—youth have unique needs that require specialized prevention,

Context and trends in the toxic drug crisis. The toxic drug crisis continues to worsen in Canada’s largest urban centres, with the unregulated drug supply becoming increasingly more toxic and deadly, ending more lives and causing life-altering harm to many people who survive poisoning events (commonly called “overdoses”).1 As the Authorities responsible for public health in the country’s three largest cities, we share the observation that Canada is in the throes of a national emergency, and that government leadership at all levels is needed to address this crisis. In all three of our jurisdictions, drug poisoning deaths have significantly increased since 2019. In Montreal, toxic drug deaths increased from 137 in 2019 to 172 in 2022 and 182 in 2023; in Toronto, suspect drug-related deaths increased from 495 in 2019 to 740 in 2022 and 733 in 2023; 2,3 and in Vancouver, deaths increased from 250 in 2019 to 578 in 2022 and 652 in 2023.4 In addition to these deaths, toxic drug poisonings are also having significant impacts

card_text: Brief Submission: Transforming the Response to BC’s Toxic drug crisis: A [ORG] Perspective Between April 2016 and April 2024, the toxic drug crisis has claimed over 14,500 lives in British Columbia (BC) (1). Despite the increase of various mainstream harm-reduction initiatives, First Nations Peoples have continued to experience disproportionate negative impacts, including toxic drug-poisoning events and deaths (2). In 2023, 17.8% of all deaths from toxic drugs in BC were First Nations, while we only represent 3.4% of the BC population (2,3). Prior to the toxic drug crisis being declared a public health emergency in 2016, the [ORG] ([ORG]) was already on the frontlines addressing this crisis, as substance use by First Nations people, along with its consequences, is related to the systemic inequities and health disparities caused by colonialism that the [ORG] works to change. We were the first health authority in BC to develop a “Framework for Action” to address the toxic drug crisis. Our role is to engage with communities and expand our public health response to the toxic drug crisis in collaboration with our health governance partners. This submission offers an overview of the toxic drug crisis from a First Nations perspective, emphasizing the unique challenges faced by First Nations communities and individuals, and addressing the negative impact of policies such as involuntary treatment and restricting decriminalization on First Nations communities. The [ORG] calls upon the Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) to include a First Nations perspective in its study on the opioid epidemic and toxic

card_text: Submission to the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Health (HESA): Prioritizing a Child and Youth Lens in the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis Study Children’s Healthcare Canada (CHC), along with our members, urges the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) to include a child and youth-centered perspective in its study on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis. Through extensive consultation with our member organizations, health providers, and leading child health experts, we recognize that this crisis has deep and far-reaching impacts on Canada’s youngest citizens—impacts that are too often overlooked in favor of adult-focused interventions. Addressing these gaps is essential, as children and youth face distinct challenges, vulnerabilities, and barriers when it comes to mental health, substance use, and addiction. We respectfully submit this brief to advocate for the inclusion of a comprehensive, evidencebased approach to children’s mental health and substance use in HESA’s study. Children’s Healthcare Canada would also be honored to convene leading child health experts to testify before the committee and provide direct insights into the unique needs of children and youth in this critical study. The Urgency of Action for Children and Youth: From our consultation with children’s healthcare providers, a clear and consistent message emerged: the current systems of care are failing children and youth. card_text: Policy Proposal: Addressing Drug Addiction through Comprehensive Legal and Supportive Measures Author: T. Lewis Federal Policy Analyst (Submitting as a Concerned Private Citizen with Indigenous family members) Date: August 9, 2024 Policy Proposal: Addressing Drug Addiction through Comprehensive Legal and Supportive Measures Author: T. Lewis Policy Analyst Executive Summary The increasing prevalence of illicit drug use and its associated societal impacts represent a pressing humanitarian crisis in Ontario. Public intoxication with illicit drugs has severe implications for individual health, community safety, and societal well-being. This policy proposal advocates for making public intoxication with illicit drugs illegal and introduces a compassionate, comprehensive diversion program that mandates treatment over punishment. The goal is to transform the current punitive approach into a rehabilitative one, emphasizing the restoration of human potential through state-of-the-art treatment facilities and robust support systems. Problem Statement Ontario's municipalities are overwhelmed by the twin crises of homelessness and drug addiction, exacerbated by inadequate and unpredictable funding from provincial and federal governments. The loss of human potential due to illicit drug use, evidenced by over 2,500 annual overdose deaths in Ontario alone, demands an urgent and multifaceted response. Current ad-hoc funding and fragmented efforts are insufficient to address the deepening crisis, necessitating a structured and compassionate approach to both law enforcement and rehabilitation. Objectives 1. Make Public Intoxication with Illicit Drugs Illegal - Introduce legislation that prohibits public intoxication with illicit drugs to enhance public safety and community well-being. 2. Implement a Compassionate Diversion Program - Develop a court diversion program

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health - “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis” A Rural Canada Perspective Prepared by Holly Mathias, MA PhD Candidate, School of Public Health, University of Alberta June 24, 2024 Introduction I am a doctoral candidate in the School of Public Health at the University of Alberta (Edmonton, AB). My research examines the experiences of family members who provide care/support for people who use drugs in rural Canada. Through my work, I collaborate with families, community organizations, frontline workers and people with lived and living experience of drug use in rural communities across the Prairie provinces. Before beginning doctoral studies, I lived in Nova Scotia, where I conducted nationally funded community-based research on access to substance use disorder treatment programs in Atlantic Canada. The aim of this brief is two-fold: 1) to synthesize the scientific evidence on drug poisonings within a rural Canadian context; and 2) to present evidence from people with lived/living experience of substance use, families, frontline workers and community members, as heard during a 2024 national webinar and workshop on drug poisonings in rural and remote Canada. Background Since 2016, over 42,000 Canadians have died from apparent opioid toxicity deaths (Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses, 2024). Between January and September 2023, it was estimated that an average of 22 Canadians died per day (Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses, 2024). I feel it is important to expand on details of the London experience as I believe that some claims that have been made are not an accurate reflection of the situation. In 2012 I recognized that there were numerous patients suffering from right-side Tricuspid Valve Infective Endocarditis. An infectious disease specialist I worked with, Dr. Kaveri Gupta, identified an increase in HIV among injection drug users despite London having a robust needle exchange program run through the Regional HIV/AIDS Connection. The number of people suffering continued to rise. In June of 2016, a public health emergency was declared by the Middlesex London Health Unit due to high rates of HIV, Hepatitis C, Infective Endocarditis (IE) and iGAS (Infectious group A strep). Infectious Disease Specialists, including the Chief of Infectious Diseases at London Health Sciences Centre, Dr. Michael Silverman and scientific researchers, including myself, were able to prove that the rates of Tricuspid Valve Infective Endocarditis were a result of injecting long-acting hydromorphone, also known as HydroMorph Contin. We demonstrated that the high rate of HIV was related to sharing injection drug use paraphernalia, specifically cookers and filters, when people were injecting long-acting hydromorphone. We met with numerous community agencies to develop a collaborative community response to address this crisis. One initiative came from London InterCommunity Health

We are members of the Youth Health Team at the BC Centre on Substance Use and University of British Columbia. We have led 15+ years of community-based research with youth who use drugs, and are pleased to contribute expertise and research evidence to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada.” Our submission highlights the substance use and associated treatment and care experiences and perspectives of young people who use drugs (YPWUD; ages 14 to 29), primarily in the context of street-based homelessness and unstable housing in British Columbia. We contribute key findings from a report titled, Youth Voices on Treatment in the Shadow of the Overdose Crisis: Key Recommendations and Findings for Care Providers which provides recommendations for youth care providers. card_text: Written Submission for the Standing Committee on Health Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada On behalf of The [ORG] May 24, 2024 Context: Towns and cities across Canada are experiencing high rates of drug overdose and death. Experts, policy makers and community service agencies have expressed their views on what needs to be done and have been working on doing it. There are polarizing views on how to solve this devastating societal issue in Canada. Any and all of the solutions are complex. Some propose better support for youth experiencing difficulties to help prevent their need to explore substances as a coping strategy. Others feel the solution is in making sure that people have a safe supply of drugs so that we can keep them alive long enough to convince them to try treatment supports, while others propose that we focus primarily on the recovery for those struggling with substance use. Likely, we need to be doing a combination of all of these things and more. Many of the solutions that we frequently hear about are based on the assumption that people using substances are fully functional when they are not using substances. This is important because programs and policy to address substance use in Canada are often built on this premise. We know that there are people with disabilities, and specifically those with Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD), who are often unrecognized, undiagnosed and unsupported. When this happens, we know that people with FASD are much more

card_text: [ORG] is an organization that provides health care to people who are homeless or vulnerably housed1. We offer a wide variety of shelter based and supportive housing-based social-determinants of health services for individuals, the vast majority of whom use drugs. Since 2001, we have been leading responses to changes in Ottawa’s unregulated, non-prescribed drugs market including the recent acceleration of changes to what we now call the toxic drug supply. It is important to understand that situation on the ground across Canada is evolving rapidly and our collective response is lagging. While the strategies of a decade ago continue to mitigate some of the harms, that mitigation has become less effective due to changes in the content and potency of this drug supply, the social safety net, brain and drug clearance physiology from aging & repeated use and in the behaviour of some of those most impacted. This is compounded by lack of meaningful investment in services to support chronic pain, substance use and mental health including complex trauma treatment. The Opioid Crisis, Phase 1: The Overprescription Crisis Only 20 years ago, there were approximately 100 people in Ottawa on methadone and one of our first roles was to advocate for improved access to methadone. This was the first wave of the opioid crisis, where opioid deaths were primarily driven by prescribed and diverted OxyContin. study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, regarding the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada Prepared by: the [ORG] March 2024 Background In response to the ongoing opioid toxicity crisis, the provision of prescribed, pharmaceutical opioids (e.g., daily dispensed immediate-release hydromorphone) as an alternative to the unregulated opioid supply—a practice known as safer opioid supply or prescribed safer supply— was introduced as a harm reduction measure. Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, the number of safer opioid supply programs have rapidly expanded across Canada due to the provision of short-term, pilot funding by Health Canada and the publication of emergency Risk Mitigation Guidance in some provinces. As safer opioid supply programs have expanded across the country, there has been an ongoing dialogue about the evidence regarding their safety and effectiveness. Given that some people will crush and inject their immediate-release hydromorphone, one concern that has been raised in recent years is whether there is a risk of injection-related infections among people accessing hydromorphone tablets through safer opioid supply programs. Evidence from peer-reviewed publications and grey literature program evaluations has grown over recent years, which has largely indicated that these programs result in improved clinical outcomes.1 In parallel, our group has continued to monitor trends in injection-related infections across Ontario (provincially and regionally).2 In this submission, we summarize the current state of evidence and knowledge regarding risks and prevalence of injection-related infections among people with opioid use disorder within the context of safer opioid supply programs. card_text: BRIEFING NOTE A Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Opioid Crisis in Canada - Intersection of MMIWG2S+ and the Opioid Crisis in Montreal Iskweu Project - Native Women’s Shelter of Montreal Janis Qavavauq-Bibeau and Laura Aguiar February 2024 Summary: The Iskweu Project began in 2017 as a response to the ongoing crisis of missing and murdered Indigenous women, girls, trans, and 2-Spirit (MMIWG2S+) people throughout Quebec. The Iskweu Project wishes to respond to an immediate need for assistance expressed by families and loved ones in the case where an Indigenous woman, girl, trans, or 2-Spirit person goes missing. Our aim is to reduce and ultimately eradicate the number of missing and murdered Indigenous women, girls, trans, and Two-Spirit people in Quebec; to offer support and ensure adequate response from institutions when someone goes missing or is murdered. We seek to reduce barriers in accessing support and receiving an adequate response from institutions that have historically discriminated against Indigenous peoples. A large part of our work is also supporting Indigenous women, girls, trans, and 2-Spirit people who are facing violence in order to prevent future disappearances or murders. Annually, we work with approximately 75 MMIWG2S+ family members and survivors throughout Quebec. Increasingly, we have observed the impact that the opioid crisis is having on Indigenous women facing violence and the broader MMIWG2S+ crisis. card_text: March 11, 2024 Standing Committee on Health House of Commons, Canada [CONTACT] Sixth Floor, [CONTACT], House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT], Canada RE: Brief – Committee Study on “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada” This brief is submitted following last week’s committee session on “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada”. In particular, the line of questioning claiming that “recreational fentanyl” is being prescribed to children (reference: Session dated Monday, February 26, 2024). The [ORG] ([ORG]) was responsible for developing the clinical protocols from which the committee member made these claims. This brief is submitted with the hope of providing clarity about these clinical resources and related public health policy to ensure recommendations made by your committee are based on fact. More information on the clinical protocols and their development is provided below. However, it is important to note that the clinical protocols being referenced do not target youth populations specifically. Additionally, the emphasis on youth access is completely untrue – no youth under the age of 19 has been prescribed fentanyl as an alternative to toxic unregulated drugs since the protocols were published. Prescribed safer supply is considered one public health intervention and harm reduction approach to help reduce or eliminate reliance on the unregulated drug supply. There are a variety of harm reduction programs and services like overdose prevention sites, naloxone and needle distribution services, which aim to reduce the negative consequences of drug use. card_text: MORE THAN 130 EXPERTS IN SUBSTANCE USE CALL FOR SCALE UP OF SAFER SUPPLY PROGRAMS December 14, 2023 The Honourable Mark Holland Minister of Health House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] The Honourable Ya’ara Saks Minister of Addiction and Mental Health House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] Dear Minister Holland and Minister Saks, As researchers and clinicians across Canada, we are writing out of concern regarding the increased politicization surrounding the response to the drug toxicity overdose crisis that is taking the lives of 21 Canadians every day. We are particularly concerned about the spread of misinformation and the denial of the evidence-base on harm reduction interventions, such as prescribed safer supply programs. The ongoing drug toxicity overdose crisis needs to be recognized as a public health emergency driven by an unregulated and volatile drug supply composed of fentanyl, fentanyl analogues and other contaminants of uncertain dose and composition. Prescribed safer supply programs aim to reduce overdose by providing regulated, pharmaceutical drugs of known potency and consistency to people who use unregulated drugs. These programs have been piloted on a small-scale since 2020 to provide a new option for people at high risk of overdose and for whom traditional treatment options like opioid agonist treatments (OAT) have not worked or provided sufficient benefit. Research demonstrates positive impacts of safer supply programs Currently, there are 24 prescribed safer supply programs across Canada receiving federal funding for operations. Context The strength and resilience of Indigenous Peoples in Canada is rooted within a rich diversity of cultures, traditions, and values that have long been nurtured by Indigenous health systems and healing practices. Despite this, Indigenous people across generations have been and continue to be deeply affected by the ongoing legacy of settler colonialism, institutionalized racism, and systemic discrimination in Canada. The final reports from the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) and the Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls (MMIWG) Inquiry demonstrated how manifestations of cultural oppression, structural violence, and abuse have resulted in long-lasting health impacts, intergenerational trauma, and economic and social marginalization for Indigenous peoples in this country. These systems of oppression have affected a range of social determinants of health, including income, education, employment, social status, working and living conditions, health practices, and child development. Hastings St, [CONTACT] Vancouver, Canada February 9th 2024 CC: the Honourable Standing Committee on Health To whom it may concern, The [ORG] ([ORG]) requests that the following brief be considered as information pertinent to your committee's ongoing study of the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada. [ORG] has a core membership of between 40 to 60 peers who are current or former illicit alcohol users living in and around the Downtown Eastside. [ORG] defines illicit drinking as the consumption of non-beverage alcohols (including mouthwash, hand sanitizer, and rubbing alcohol) and the use of beverage alcohol in ways that are criminalized, most often due to coerced use of public space due to homelessness, discrimination by service providers, or residence in a Single Room Occupancy (SRO) hotel. [ORG] members are usually long-term, heavy drinkers who meet established criteria for Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) and who are likely to experience high-risk withdrawal symptoms, including seizures and Delirium Tremens. Illicit drinkers across the community experience incredibly high rates of alcohol and drug-related harm including but not limited to drug poisoning death, accidental injury, and interactions with law enforcement, and hepatic injury. Over 80% of [ORG] members are Indigenous, and experience high rates of alcohol-associated harm intersecting with socioeconomic marginality and intergenerational experience of colonial violence. The majority of our members are currently Managed Alcohol Program (MAP) clients, live on extremely low incomes, and have not benefited from abstinence-oriented models of care. EIDGEs

card_text: Friday, January 26, 2024 Standing Committee on Health House of Commons, Canada [CONTACT] Sixth Floor, [CONTACT], House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT], Canada ATTN: Christine Sing, Clerk of the Standing Committee on Health RE: Brief – Committee Study on “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada” As university researchers with expertise in substance use and drug policy, we are pleased to contribute evidence to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada.” Our submission draws attention to inequities in opioid use and overdose among Two-Spirit, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and other queer (2S/LGBTQ+) youth and offers direction for responsive policy and practice action. This submission leverages findings and recommendations from our team’s past and ongoing research on opioid and other substance use among 2S/LGBTQ+ youth. Specifically, we share insights from a commentary publication about overdose among 2S/LGBTQ+ people, and from two published articles related to our qualitative studies with 2S/LGBTQ+ youth (ages 15 to 30) who use drugs, one of which focuses on overdose and harm reduction, and the other on opioid use. These studies and our broader programs of research have emphasized the need to: improve access to harm reduction and other non-judgmental, inclusive, and low-barrier substancerelated health services; and address the root drivers of the drug poisoning crisis by decriminalizing substance use and providing a safer drug supply, using various models. I have practiced as an RN in Ontario and in Emergency Departments in both Duncan and Nanaimo on Vancouver Island, BC. As a part of my current role, I teach nursing students in surgical and mental health acute care settings. I have been an advocate for people who use drugs in my community since 2018 and have served as the Chair of the Nanaimo Community Action Team on the Toxic Drug Crisis (CAT). I am also a proud member of the Harm Reduction Nurses Association. My community of Nanaimo has been absolutely devastated by the unregulated drug crisis. Since the provincial declaration of the overdose crisis as a public health emergency in 2016, there have been over 417 deaths in our community (as of October 31, 2023, BC Coroners Service). According to Dr. Shannon Waters, Interim Central Island Medical Health Officer, 85 people died in Nanaimo in 2022 and that rate has been increasing exponentially with over 99 people dying from an unregulated supply of drugs in just the first 10 months of 2023. Introduction The [ORG] promotes the human rights of people living with HIV or AIDS and other populations disproportionately affected by HIV, punitive laws and policies, and criminalization, in Canada and internationally. For three decades, we have advocated for drug policy grounded in evidence, human rights, and public health. The [ORG] works collaboratively with governments, affected communities, and civil society to improve community health and safety by conducting research and outreach on effective and evidence-based policy responses to substance use. We are grateful for the opportunity to make this joint submission detailing key legal determinants of health that have spurred the toxic drug crisis. II. Criminalization and Canada’s Drug Poisoning Crisis Canada’s approach to drugs, focused primarily on prohibition rather than prevention, harm reduction, and support for people who use drugs, has done catastrophic harm, fuelling deadly stigma, racism, epidemics of preventable illness and death, and widespread, egregious violations of human rights. card_text: BRIEF RECOMMENDATIONS TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH ON THE OPIOID EPIDEMIC AND TOXIC DRUG CRISIS IN CANADA Amber Streukens January 15, 2024 This brief is submitted on behalf of the Rural Empowered Drug Users Network (REDUN), a grassroots support, education, and advocacy group of people with lived and living experience (PWLLE) of substance use meeting in the West Kootenay region of BC. With organizational support from [ORG], a longstanding leader in rural harm reduction - REDUN hosts biweekly meetings in 3 communities, provides peer led harm reduction services, and advocates for the needs of rural PWLLE. Despite high rates of injury and fatality in rural regions (estimated at 48.5/100,000 in the Kootenay Boundary HSDA in 2023i), access to harm reduction and recovery services is still extremely limited outside of urban centers. People who use substances (PWUS) in the Kootenay region have inadequate access to: opioid agonist therapy (OAT), pharmaceutical alternatives to the toxic drug supply, drug checking, overdose prevention services, withdrawal management, inpatient treatment, recovery services, supportive housing, relapse prevention, and supportive housing. No community is untouched by this crisis. It is imperative that the federal government take immediate, concrete action to reduce harms across the country. Approaching elections, it seems this issue has moved from the realm of public health into political theater. This is unacceptably dehumanizing and poses a serious threat to human life. Ideology has no place in emergency public health response. Individuals and communities in crisis must not be sacrificed for political

Name: “Innovating Beyond Exclusively Medicalized Approaches” Policy Brief and Recommendations Updated February 2023
org: Canadian Civil Society Advancing Safe Supply Working Group
study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: “Innovating Beyond Exclusively Medicalized Approaches” Policy Brief and Recommendations Updated February 2023 Executive Summary • Amidst a nation-wide illegal drug toxicity1 crisis, pharmaceutical-grade alternatives to the illegal drug supply (referred to henceforth as “safe supply”) are an effective intervention for mitigating drug policy- related harms. • Medicalized models of safe supply produce positive impacts for some people who use drugs (PWUD) and the broader public. • Individual benefits for PWUD participating in safe supply include reductions in illegal drug use, overdose, and criminalized behaviour, and increased positive health and housing outcomes. • Community benefits of safe supply include reduction in health care utilization and associated health care system costs, among others. • However, the reach of existing prescriber-driven, or medicalized, safe supply programs is highly limited; as such, they are not an adequate standalone response to the urgent drug toxicity crisis. Medicalized approaches also present barriers and exclusions for racialized and other marginalized populations. • Precariously funded pilot studies are impermanent and unsustainable. • Medicalized safe supply programs are mired in capacity, institutional, and guideline constraints, accommodating only a fraction of the hundreds of thousands who would benefit from legal access to a pharmaceutical-grade supply of drugs. • The requirement that one be labeled with a “substance use disorder” to access medicalized safe supply exacerbates drug-related stigma. • Medicalized safe supply is not available to those who use episodically, despite this cohort being at the highest risk for drug poisoning given their lack of drug tolerance. • Medicalized safe supply

study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: Submission to HESA Committee Study on ‘Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis’ Prepared by: [ORG] January 10 2024 The Toxic Drug Crisis is Driven by the Illegality and Lack of Regulation of Currently Illicit Drugs Contrary to dominant narratives, rates of drug use and rates of substance use disorders (SUD) have not increased in Canada over the last decade. Nonetheless, rates of drug-related fatalities and hospitalizations have skyrocketed during this period. The overwhelming driver of this crisis is the prohibition of drugs under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act (CDSA). Prohibition has not decreased availability or use of illicit drugs but has caused continuous shifts in Canada’s unregulated drug supply. It has created a variable and volatile drug market that poses imminent risk of fatal and non-fatal overdose to all people who consume drugs, including those who use episodically or recreationally. People are now unknowingly consuming drugs that contain multiple, often novel, substances as the direct consequence of enforcement initiatives that cause drug producers and sellers to introduce ever-more substances and potent analogues into the supply to evade detection. Solutions to the toxic drug crisis must therefore be oriented toward the responsible regulation of the drug supply. Between January 2016 and June 2023 in Canada (excluding Quebec) there were 40,642 apparent opioid toxicity deaths, now averaging 22 deaths per day, and 39,435 opioid-related and 16,863 stimulant-related poisoning hospitalizations. From January to June 2023, 80% of opioid deaths involved fentanyl from the unregulated supply, a 21% increase since 2018. While this

card_text: Submissions to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, regarding the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada December 21, 2023 Prepared by Safiyya Ahmad, Staff Counsel Introduction The BC Civil Liberties Association (“BCCLA”) frequently engages in work that intersects with drug policy and the drug poisoning crisis, due to the overlap between these issues and the criminal legal system, Charter rights, and human rights impacts on marginalized communities. We appreciate this opportunity to provide our perspective on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis (the “Drug Poisoning Crisis”), to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (the “Committee”). The BCCLA is concerned that there has been insufficient focus on the extent to which criminal law and civil liberties issues have heightened the Drug Poisoning Crisis. In the submissions below, we address three key concerns: 1. The criminalization of drug use significantly exacerbates this crisis, due to dangers of arrest, stigmatization, and social support barriers. 2. Police presence during wellness checks frequently results in violence, which deters people from calling emergency services for assistance. 3. Involuntary treatment is a violation of drug users’ civil liberties and increases the risk of death. Beyond these submissions, we acknowledge that resolving the Drug Poisoning Crisis will require a holistic approach, centering evidence‐based research as well as the knowledge and experience of people who use drugs. Name: Memorandum – Nathalie Vaillant: If I Had Known Submitted to:
card_text: Memorandum – Nathalie Vaillant: If I Had Known Submitted to: Standing Committee on Health (SCH) Subject: Monitoring of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) 30 April 2023 PREAMBULE Quebec’s motto is “Je me souviens” . . . well, mine is: IF I HAD KNOWN INTRODUCTION This memorandum is being submitted without a presentation and will describe all the struggles I have had that could have been avoided . . . IF I HAD KNOWN, IF I HAD BEEN CLEARLY INFORMED. If you’re in good health, why be afraid of the word cancer? In your family, you have one sister who had breast cancer very young; you’re not immune. You perform your breast self-exam and find a small lump. You tell yourself, “Nah, not me, that can’t be!” When you see your treating physician and ask for every test imaginable to rule out any likelihood of bad news and she tells you, “Just because your sister had cancer doesn’t mean you’ll get it.” So again, I am asking the question: Why be afraid of the word cancer? When you read my history, you will notice that all the surgeries I’ve undergone are in red, to highlight the importance of monitoring medical devices. Name: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Medical Device Monitoring (Breast Implants) [CONTACT] Hello,
card_text: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Medical Device Monitoring (Breast Implants) [CONTACT] Hello, In October 2014, I was diagnosed with cancer in my right breast. In January 2015, after starting chemotherapy treatments, I was told that I should consider a right-side mastectomy. I was shocked at this point. I thought my cancer had been removed and that I undergoing preventive treatment. During several meetings with the oncologist and radiation oncologist, it was decided that a bilateral mastectomy would be preferable for various reasons. The oncologist had me meet with the plastic surgeon because she could offer me several types of reconstruction. There was never any question that staying flat would be an option. The plastic surgeon met with me and advised me that my body situation required implants. That the only risks associated with implants were rejection and the usual risks associated with anaesthesia. I was not informed that the textured implants, the ones I received, brought with them a risk of cancer or of capsular contracture. Nor was I told that I should change them every 10 years. That I should have more regular follow-up because I had implants. I learned from a television program about the health risks implants can cause. That they can be associated, not with only one cancer, but with two and now three cancers. My implants were removed in October 2019, to my great relief. The reason for my participation in this brief on the urgency of having a breast implant

Name: Memoirs - Annie - 40 years old - implanted in 2012 - withdrawn in 2019
card_text: Memoirs - Annie - 40 years old - implanted in 2012 - withdrawn in 2019 For me, everything started with the Enquête broadcast. At that time, I had been having lots of headaches for nearly two years. I cried a lot when I watched that show. Maybe that’s why I’m having these headaches. Then I had lots of questions: What will I look like if I have my implants removed? It must be very expensive. How will I be able to pay for that? And what if that’s not even the reason for my headaches? I buried my head in the sand for a few months. But the constant headaches convinced me I had to do something. Maybe these foreign bodies were poisoning my life! A lot of my friends with implants say they’re going to wait to see if something happens... OK, but once the problem has fully manifested itself, by the time you realize it might be your implants, it could be irreversible.... Do you wait for the brakes on your car to stop working before you change them? No, because you could have a serious accident! We should not put our lives in danger! Another comparison I like is that reusable water bottles in stores are BPA-free because apparently, drinking from plastic is not healthy.... Implants, plastic pouches (plus a ton of heavy metals) are in you 24/7! Mentor silicone implants 425/450cc implanted in 2012. I had them removed on October 24, 2019. Symptoms: - Headaches -

Name: BRIEF My story in brief I had rhinoplasty and breast augmentation at the age of 23 on May 25, 1987, performed by Dr. Yvon Ménard, a plastic surgeon. I started to experience a variety of problems withi
card_text: BRIEF My story in brief I had rhinoplasty and breast augmentation at the age of 23 on May 25, 1987, performed by Dr. Yvon Ménard, a plastic surgeon. I started to experience a variety of problems within the first few months, including dizziness, feeling unsteady on my feet and constant and severe vertigo. Today is May 5, 2023, and at the age of 60, the problem is getting worse, and the implants are still in place 35 years later. My health is deteriorating, and in 2019, I received a diagnosis that the implants had ruptured. I have been waiting for the past five years to have TOTAL CAPSULECTOMY surgery to remove them in order to regain my health. MODEL: REPLICON silicon breast implants CATALOGUE: 2-155 SIZE: 2 WEIGHT: 155 grams CONTROL #513327 and #545202 I urge the government and Health Canada to take prompt action to establish a confidential registry for all women who have had breast augmentation, and for these women to be heard.

card_text: I am immunodeficient. I was diagnosed at a very young age. In 2016, I was convinced I had cancer. Lymphoma. The lymph nodes in my neck were severely enlarged and felt hard in some places. I had bouts of mild fever and night sweats. I would wake up at night, soaked in sweat and shivering like a leaf. I had lost my spark. I oŌen felt listless. I had become someone I didn’t recognize. I was losing a lot of hair. We had been trying to have our litle girl for five years, but I couldn’t get pregnant. My periods were irregular. I consulted an internist. I felt like I was at death’s door. I felt a desperate need to stay alive. I felt old. I really felt old for my age. I was so scared for her! But I had a whole team behind me to get through this ordeal. She’s doing well now. What a relief! But my own health has goten much worse. By 2021, I could hardly get through the day. Fortunately, when my maternity leave ended, I was on telework. In 2022, we travelled to Maine on vacation and I got sick. Name: To the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) To ensure that Health Canada can effect change. To ensure that the government does what needs to be don
card_text: To the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) To ensure that Health Canada can effect change. To ensure that the government does what needs to be done. To ensure that breast implant manufacturers recognize their role and take responsibility. To ensure that women’s lives and their health are taken seriously. If car parts can be recalled, medical devices like breast implants can be too. May 2005. I, Anouk, was 24, I had just earned my Bachelor of Education degree, and I decided to put $5,000 on my very first credit card, not only so I could finally feel like a real woman, but also to reward myself for four years of hard work at university. I bought myself breast implants. Nothing excessive, just enough to look feminine. The surgeon I met recommended smooth saline implants—apparently the best implants on the market, with no risks, unlike silicone implants. Worst case scenario, if there was a leak, there would be nothing to worry about because the implants are filled with a saline solution. No threat to my body. They could simply be changed. A few hours after the operation, I headed home. Along with the printed post-op instructions, I also found a card, like a proof of warranty. It was the Device Identification Card. It included the name of the company, the model of the implants, and the volume of saline solution implanted. INAMED, McGHAN Style 68. 240 CC left breast, same for the right breast.

Name: Dominique Lévesque 42 years old Implants: Smooth round moderate profile gel breast implant cohesive 300cc #350-7300BC Batch: 5629023 and 5653215
card_text: Dominique Lévesque 42 years old Implants: Smooth round moderate profile gel breast implant cohesive 300cc #350-7300BC Batch: 5629023 and 5653215 Implanted on 2006-05-31 Explanted on 2023-03-28 Hello, I had Mentor smooth silicone implants 300cc from May 31, 2006, to March 28, 2023. I was 26 years old at the time of the operation and I had several health problems following this implantation. Like many women in my situation, I have experienced health problems throughout my almost 17 years of living with my implants. This exhaustive list is minimal compared to some of us. Here are some of the most striking symptoms: chronic fatigue for the first two years, inflammation, joint problems, muscle pain, night sweats from the age of 28-30, gastrointestinal problems, liver problems, allergies and food intolerances that developed without warning, repeated sinusitis. All of these problems exist otherwise, but here they only have one thing in common: all women with breast implants notice these symptoms. Is this a coincidence? Of course not! Knowing all this now, I can only hope that Health Canada can properly advise women who will have to choose to have breast implants in their lives. These toxic sacks poison more women than we think and some manufacturers do not follow the evolution of their product over time to see all these problems that are closely related to it. In fact, many physicians agree that there is certainly some negligence. Some say that the link is not there, yet we have been guinea pigs since

Name: Saint-Colomban, May 4, 2023 To whom it may concern: In 2007, at the age of 29, I thought I had made the right decision. I had a breast augmentation that I thought was safe. Despite the kindness of the
card_text: Saint-Colomban, May 4, 2023 To whom it may concern: In 2007, at the age of 29, I thought I had made the right decision. I had a breast augmentation that I thought was safe. Despite the kindness of the surgeon, Dr. Jacques Papillon, we were misled to believe that the implants were safe. If a registry had existed, I would have made a better choice for my health. Had there been a registry, thousands of women would have been contacted in one way or another more officially than through a support group. Why does Canada approve these implants and then turn a blind eye? Enough is enough! It is high time for this to change. It is Health Canada’s responsibility to implement a medical implant registry that would allow patients to be traced and notified immediately if problems arise. Automakers have a registry—good on them! France has a registry—bravo! ...So why not us? I never would have gone through this if I had known. Women must have the right to know the risks and impacts on their health. About three years after the augmentation, I noticed abnormal hair loss that got worse over the years. Other symptoms appeared, like fatigue, and sometimes I had to take two naps during the day. I’ve also experienced dry eyes and joint problems. Thanks to the support group, I realized that I was far from alone and that my case was not the worst. I decided to have my implants removed after 15 years

Name: To: Members of the Standing Committee (HESA) SUBMISSION ON A REGISTRY FOR MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS) The following submission details my personal story with Breast Implants and the reasons why
card_text: To: Members of the Standing Committee (HESA) SUBMISSION ON A REGISTRY FOR MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS) The following submission details my personal story with Breast Implants and the reasons why we need a registry. In 2009, I had surgery to place Allergan textured implants (under MSP), after a double mastectomy. The only advice given at the time was my implants had a warranty of 10 years. Since the surgery, I have increasingly been unwell, with most symptoms seeming to relate to Breast Implant Illness (BII) as well as capsular contracture and pain. In my long search for medical answers for being so ill, I was ridiculed by the medical system due to a lack of knowledge. Late in 2021 while continuing to search for answers, I found a Breast Implant Illness site that mentioned implant recalls. Mine were noted as being recalled in 2019 due to incidences of BIA-ALCL cancer. I immediately contacted my original plastic surgeon and was initially brushed off. Later this surgeon indicated he must have missed me on the recall, despite still being at the same address and phone number. He stated he even involved the Interior Health Authority for assistance in his search for recalled patients. He made light of BII and removal and said many of his patients chose to leave them in, perhaps due to this minimization. I also contacted Health Canada for confirmation of the recall. Several letters and several months later (necessitating involving my MP), I finally received recall confirmation. Not

Name: Dear Members of Parliament,
card_text: Dear Members of Parliament, I am writing to you to share my personal experience with having textured Mentor breast implants for 14 years in hopes this may urge you to consider establishing a Breast Implant Registry in Canada. I hope that my story will serve as a testimony to the need for a comprehensive database to track breast implant-related complications and adverse events. Within 6 months of implantation, I developed questionable lumps that required monitoring every 6 months via mammograms and ultrasounds. As time went on, I started experiencing digestive issues, ongoing rashes, vision issues, headaches, neck and shoulder pain, intense brain fog, chest pains, and difficulty breathing. These symptoms also caused multiple bouts of depression. Despite visiting multiple doctors and specialists and being told that everything was fine and perhaps all was stress related, I finally felt that my underlying issues were finally validated after receiving a letter from my implanting surgeon outlining the symptoms of Breast Implant Illness (BII) associated with my breast implants. My immediate reaction was to make an appointment with him to discuss the letter and have the implants removed. Unfortunately, my visit was unwelcome, and I was dismissed as the letter was only sent out of obligation and not because of his belief in BII. He told me that I would look disfigured by removing them and that he honestly did not believe they were causing my issues. Foolishly, I followed his advice and left the implants in. As time passed, my symptoms worsened

card_text: Mercier, May 2, 2023 Hello, I am a 39-year-old woman who had implants for eight years. In April 2014 I got the Mentor smooth round moderate profile saline 330cc implants ref: 3501645 SN: 6739990-049 and 6739990-076. I had my breast implants removed one year ago. I had to undergo surgery twice on my right breast during the first year of implantation because my right implant had a capsular contraction. Physiological inflammatory reaction of the body. I had a lot of disabling symptoms (fatigue, joint pain, digestive problems, memory problems, anxiety, night sweats, hormonal imbalance, brain fog, burning eyes, sensitivity to sound, no longer able to work out, I had to stop the work I was passionate about because my body could no longer keep up, etc.). Most of my symptoms, however, have disappeared if not greatly improved since the implants were removed. I find it very important to have an up-to-date registry not only for cancers related to breast implants that are new, but also because in my case, I had a recall on my implants and I was never contacted by the cosmetic surgery clinic that put my implants in eight years ago. It is not normal that we have recalls on our cars, but not on supposedly safe objects implanted in our bodies. In short, I sincerely hope that this will change, it is important for all people who have such surgeries. Stéphanie Dupuis

Name: Good morning to all members of the HESA Committee, After observing the meetings of the past weeks, I felt compelled to write to all of you. Thank you for giving us a voice and a platform to bring fort
card_text: Good morning to all members of the HESA Committee, After observing the meetings of the past weeks, I felt compelled to write to all of you. Thank you for giving us a voice and a platform to bring forth our concerns. My name is Dawn Criss, and I am a BIA ALCL survivor, a BII patient and a member of the advocacy team alongside Ms. McGregor, Ms. Elliott and Ms. Pratt. I was the first BIA ALCL patient diagnosed in Alberta in 2018 and I am also a patient of Dr. Tervaert. It seems widely agreed upon, that an implant registry is long overdue. However, had we incorporated a registry when implants were first introduced to the market 60 years ago, we would have had enough data to contact patients with recalls of defective products, recalls for BIA ALCL and other cancers and also to pull data for our own Canadian studies. To start from scratch now, it will take many years to gather what’s required to accomplish any of these tasks. Perhaps we could find a way for patients AND plastic surgeons to add previous surgical/pathological data and adverse reaction events into the new registry, allowing us to grow the database much quicker? This registry should be an "opt out" database similar to an excel spreadsheet, where all information can eventually be sorted and filtered to pull data for any study required. Personal and private patient information should be hidden so that access can ONLY be made by the

card_text: Medical devices oversight committee (breast implants)] My submission on my breast implants From a dream to a hard reality! “Why didn’t God give me breasts? I don’t feel like a woman or desirable with my small breasts. I swear that one day I will end up getting a pair!” These were the thoughts that ran through my mind every day, every time I looked in the mirror when I woke up. Name: Standing Committee on Health (HESA), Luc Thériault, MP for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Health Committee
card_text: Standing Committee on Health (HESA), Luc Thériault, MP for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Health Committee Study: “OVERSIGHT OF MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS)” Dear Mr. Thériault: I am in favour of a national breast implant registry, and I am calling on you to create one, both for women today and for future generations of women. Health Canada is responsible for approving health products for Canadians. A registry is needed to warn women about complications involving their implants. Patients could be identified and immediately notified in the event of a problem. More importantly, let’s not forget that all types of implants can be affected by new cancers. A 2019 news article said that 15,000 women in Quebec who had textured implants would be contacted by authorities. Members of the group with textured implants then told us that no one had ever contacted them and that it was only via the group that they found out they should have been contacted by the hospital or by the surgeon who had performed their textured implant surgery. If there had been a registry for medical devices (breast implants), these women would have likely been contacted in a more official way than via a support group. Car manufacturers manage to do it, and there are way more cars on the road than there are breast implants! Personally, I got breast implants in March 2000 and had them removed in February 2023 after seeing reports about breast implant illness. They were foreign objects inside my body, and

Name: Support for a Quebec silicon implants register
card_text: Support for a Quebec silicon implants register My story goes back to 1979 when I was a young 18-year-old mother who breastfed her child until the age of three. At the age of 22, I went for a “boob job,” which consisted of a breast lift and silicon gel implants to have a normal bust, because three years of breastfeeding had caused some damage – my breasts were flat and empty and in 1983, cosmetic surgery was very popular and accessible. I found the best surgeon in Montreal, and this doctor performed my cosmetic surgery from 1983 to 2015. The first surgery went well; I was still quite young, and these were my first implants. I remember going back to see the surgeon three months after surgery because the left side was hard and painful. A shell had formed, and this was not good. The doctor broke and crushed the shell with both hands, and he was satisfied. It was very painful, but the shell did not form again. At the time, I was told that this was a normal complication. I gave birth to two girls in 1994 and 1995 at the age of 32 and 34, respectively. I breastfed my babies while the old implants were still in place. I was lucky to conceive and to be able to breastfeed my beautiful children without a second thought about the implants or possible adverse effects on my health as a mother. Name: Cynthia Bizier I, Cynthia Bizier, am writing to you to present my experience with breast implants and the need for a registry. On October 9, 2013, at the age of 25, I got Mentor saline Smooth High Pro
card_text: Cynthia Bizier I, Cynthia Bizier, am writing to you to present my experience with breast implants and the need for a registry. On October 9, 2013, at the age of 25, I got Mentor saline Smooth High Profile 380cc breast implants filled to 420cc. I was in good health and exercising five to six times a week. I was not on any medication. My health began a subtle decline a month after surgery. First, when I resumed my regular workouts, my right wrist started aching immediately afterward. I even saw a doctor when the pain did not subside, and the doctor diagnosed tendinitis on the right wrist and told me to rest. And yet, I waited six weeks to resume exercises, as the surgeon had told me. I did not have any history of injury or weakness on this joint. I took antiinflammatories for three weeks and got a wrist splint and obviously, I did not do any workouts. I was very sad because working out was an integral part of my life. Later, I tried to use my cardio workout DVDs a month after the tendinitis to avoid putting weight on my wrists, but this time, my shoulders started to act up. Therefore, with a heavy heart, I decided to stop all physical activity because of bouts of inflammation in my joints. I thought things would get better with time, that I had probably not recovered from the breast implant. I started to suffer from depression because I could

card_text: Venise-en Québec, May 4, 2023 [CONTACT] Oversight of medical devices (breast implants) To whom it may concern, My name is Olivette d’Anjou. It took me several months to heal, not counting all of the pain. I had two children to take care of and with all the work I had to do at home, I had to give up my job outside the home. A few months later, I became pregnant and the problems really started after giving birth. I was constantly exhausted, needed to sleep several times a day, had muscle and bone pain, headaches, stomach aches, my feet were also numb and splotchy, and more! After about two years, I asked to see the specialist again, but it was not the same one who had done the operation, and I had to go to Quebec City to—I do not know what the term is, but it was like bursting the shell around the breasts, which was very painful. card_text: May 5, 2023 Dear members of the committee, First, I would like to thank you for receiving the breast implant registry case. This letter is particularly dear to my heart for several reasons. I wore Mentor brand cohesive gel breast implants with a smooth surface for 12 years. Over the years I have had many side effects such as hair loss, insomnia, dehydration, itching, dizziness, sun intolerance, digestive problems, extreme fatigue, decreased menstruation, memory loss, difficulty in articulating, pale complexion and no longer tanning, inability to do more than one thing at a time, capsular contracture that required two additional surgeries... and I’m sure I forgot some others. The first implants were placed on January 16, 2008, and cost me $6,153.30. From the very first weeks I had insomnia. Then, due to a capsular contracture in my left breast, the scar tissue started to squeeze the implant and cause pain as a result. I had to undergo surgery at a cost of about $3,000, if memory serves. The surgery consisted of opening the capsule to enlarge it, but first the implant had to be removed, which my surgeon did, but she broke it during the procedure. Surely when it broke, cohesive gel flowed into my body. Although she cleaned and sponged it all off, the body has absorbent tissues, so for sure she was unable to remove all the silicone gel. In the weeks that followed, memory loss began and so did joint problems. On October 19, 2015, I had

Name: Mr. Luc Thériault Member of Parliament for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Commitee on Health Standing Commitee on Health (HESA)
card_text: Mr. Luc Thériault Member of Parliament for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Commitee on Health Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) The need for a national breast implant registry in Canada Dear Mr. Thériault, I am writing to express my strong support for the establishment of a centralized national breast implant registry, administered by the government, under the OVERSIGHT OF MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS) commitee. Breast implants are class IV medical devices representing the products with the highest risk potential. (1) Oversight should be conducted accordingly. - These products create a risk of a lymphoma that is specific to breast implants, BIA-ALCL. (2) Patients should be notified quickly and formally, not be made to stumble on the information in a support group. - Other issues seem to be related to breast implants and are constantly being added. (3) If it turns out that BII (Breast Implant Illness) or another condition finally becomes medically recognized, carriers will need to be notified promptly also to make an option of discussions with their treating physician. - In particular, the registry would act as a surveillance tool for pathologies related to breast implant use. It would be used to advise patients of the symptoms to watch for and to follow up with their physician, if necessary. - Health Canada has no way of determining how many patients in Canada have received these types of implants. - Automotive manufacturers notify their customers of potential problems even if they are rare (4), yet nothing is done about devices

card_text: To the attention of: Standing Committee on Health Luc Thériault, M.P., Montcalm Vice Chair of the Standing Committee on Health Hello. My name is Isabelle Tremblay. I’m 44 years old. Here is my little story. I received my implants in June 2009 and had them removed on Friday, April 28, 2023. I am one of those who decided to travel abroad for surgery. In 2009, through a Quebec medical tourism company in my city, I travelled to Tunisia for an abdominoplasty and breast augmentation. This Quebec company, which has since gone out of business, provided support during the preoperative period through to my return. Afterward, nothing. I had been wearing textured implants for nearly 14 years without knowing it. Although this was my choice and I own it, I had to deal with multiple symptoms, questions, and emotions regarding my implants, without any support. My symptoms were initially mild, and I never made any cause-and-effect connection because in my mind, my implants were not hazardous for my health. I am at heart an athletic woman who believes in healthy living and healthy eating. The first obvious sign was fatigue; however, since I had two young children at the time, I had no other reasons for my fatigue. I then began having recurring infections and yeast infections (twice a month), and very frequent cold sores (6 per year). I still take medication regularly for both these issues. Name: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief Concerning the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) Introduction
card_text: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief Concerning the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) Introduction I would like to thank the members of the Committee for the invitation to submit this brief so I can provide my views regarding the oversight of breast implants. Evolution of breast implants Between January 1992 and October 20, 2006, Health Canada imposed a moratorium on silicone implants to protect the health of all Canadian women and to ensure that these products are safe. These implants were withdrawn from the Canadian market due to safety concerns and the fact that they cause serious health problems for women, including cancer, connective tissue diseases and autoimmune diseases. Thirty years later, we are still having the same debates, the same health challenges and the same doubts vis-à-vis the safety of these products. Anaplastic large cell lymphoma cases are trending higher, as are incidences of other breast implant-related diseases. The time for oversight has passed—it is now time for action. Stronger oversight measures, in the form of a medical devices registry, should have been put in place at the appropriate time, when the moratorium on silicone breast implants was imposed in 1992. This would have made it possible to track all breast implants and maximize oversight for Canadian women. The moratorium and the Special Access Program Lack of a “breast implant registry” resulted in ineffective oversight, prevention and safety considerations. Even though Health Canada did not approve these prostheses during the moratorium, it did provide Canadian surgeons

Name: Dear M. Luc Thériault and the members of the Standing Committee on Health,
card_text: Dear M. Luc Thériault and the members of the Standing Committee on Health, I am sharing with you my utterly frightening and horrific experience with Allergan’s textured implants in order to help underline the urgency and necessity of a breast implant registry in Canada. In 2017, I consulted with a plastic surgeon, Dr. Arthur Swift, to ask what could be done about my pronounced loss of breast tissue after more than 2 years of breastfeeding my son. He recommended Allergan textured implants and so on September 11, 2017, I had breast implant surgery (410 MM, lots 2826554 & 2816784, SN 20785345 & 20706518). At no time was I informed that in 2011, the FDA announced a POSSIBLE association between textured implants and Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (ALCL) and that by 2016, a DEFINITE association was established and became widely known in the medical community. Not long after my surgery I heard a report on the news about the dangers of textured implants and asked my surgeon about it. He reassured me that the incidence of the lymphoma was estimated to be only one in 1 million. So I did my best to push it out of my mind since the surgery was over and done with. Fast forward to early March 2022 when I listened to a Montreal radio host speaking about her experience with breast implants. She talked about Breast Implant Illness and how she suffered from a myriad of symptoms such as skin rashes, brain fog, headaches, difficulty

card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by Terri McGregor, Expert BIA-ALCL Advocate | May 2023 HESA Submission – Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) The [ORG] ([ORG]) is an organization of patient advocates and knowledge experts that work with patients and leaders in Canada, the US and all over the world. We are pleased to have the opportunity to be a voice for patients and consumers and share our valuable insight from our lived-experience and professional training with members of the Standing Committee on Health. With limited time and resources, this brief was prepared by Terri McGregor, a BIA-ALCL survivor Expert BIA-ALCL Patient Advocate and part of the [ORG] core leadership team as a result of collaborative efforts with members of [ORG] including Canadian Representative Julie Elliott and Canadian Patient Advocate Nancy Pratt, founder of the BIFI Society of Canada and co-founder of the Canadian Breast Implant Failure and Illness support group. Breast implants are not life saving devices and thus there should be a very low tolerance for any risk to health, especially risks that impose huge costs on the healthcare system. There is no informed consent without data. Healthcare delivery and costs cannot be optimized without data. We appreciate that the House of Common Standing Committee on Health is taking steps to examine the feasibility of creating a nationwide registry to advance our common goal of patient safety.
