Name: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health May 2023
card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health May 2023 Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) [ORG] is grateful for the opportunity to provide its recommendations on the oversight of breast implants. [ORG] is a neutral, not-for profit organization that creates and maintains global supply chain standards in collaboration with GS1 Member Organizations in 116 countries (we are often best known for the global barcode standard). Through a federated model, collectively, we help millions of companies around the world use common identifiers on their products, locations, and services, to enable traceability and recalls, inventory management, and visibility within the supply chain. The GS1 global supply chain standards already play a significant role in the oversight of breast implants, globally. Due to regulatory requirements from other jurisdictions such the US and EU, the majority of breast implants that come to, or are made-in, Canada, already have GS1 identifiers (ie: a global barcode) on its product. This acts as a unique device identifier (UDI) for the product that is recognized globally. Therefore, Canada is already starting with a strong foundation for proper oversight of breast implants including for traceability capabilities, recalls and a data-rich registry critical for research and analytics. We are pleased to be currently engaged with the Medical Devices Directorate within Health Canada to support in the development of an optimal UDI policy for Canada. Name: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2023
card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2023 HESA Submission – Oversight of medical devices (breast implants) The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the only national charity supporting Canadians with all cancers in communities across the country. As the voice for Canadians who care about cancer, we are pleased to share with members of the Standing Committee on Health background information on breast cancer in Canada and how breast implants play a role in the recovery of people living with breast cancer. It is estimated that about 1 in 8 Canadian women will develop breast cancer during their lifetime and 1 in 34 will die from it.1 Overview of breast cancer in Canada It is estimated that in 2022: 28,600 Canadian women will be diagnosed with breast cancer. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board Submitted by [ORG] (formerly Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada) Introduction
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board Submitted by [ORG] (formerly Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada) Introduction [ORG], formerly the Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada, is pleased to provide this submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB). MS impacts all Canadians – not only individuals living with the disease, but also their families. The unpredictable, episodic, and progressive nature of MS makes it significantly challenging for many people to maintain a similar quality of life they had prior to diagnosis. For Canadians living with MS, timely and affordable access to disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) is vital to increase their quality of life by delaying disability progression and improving health outcomes. As Canada emerges from the pandemic amidst a significantly strained healthcare system, Canadians living with MS are faced with additional challenges, including barriers to accessing life-altering DMTs. Over the past six years, [ORG] has sent eight submissions in response to the PMPRB’s proposed amendments. Our position has consistently been that any amendments must take into consideration the impact on people living with MS, as we postulate that PMPRB amendments could hinder affordability and availability of disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) for people living with MS, as well as research and clinical trials for future medications. card_text: June 14, 2023 Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Subject: Study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB) Reforms Dear Honourable Members of the Standing Committee on Health, The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the national alliance of over 100 rare disease patient organizations and representing nearly 3 million Canadians affected by rare diseases. From the outset of the PMPRB reform process, our position has been clear. we support improving affordability of rare disease medicines while simultaneously assuring appropriate and timely access. However, the changes proposed by PMPRB, starting in 2017, have been singularly focused on one objective, that is, driving prices as low as possible, going far beyond even their original mandate of assuring prices were not excessive as an abuse of patent and in comparison to other countries. This approach would have had a devastating impact on Canadians living with rare diseases, not only delaying access to new medicines but actually denying their availability to Canadian patients, starting with the decline or delay of clinical trials taking place in Canadian sites. In this context, [ORG] and other stakeholders have raised numerous concerns and put forward thoughtful alternative proposals for how to address the federal government’s medicine affordability objectives without harming the access environment for new medicines. In too many cases, these concerns have largely fallen on deaf ears. We have consistently warned that the PMPRB’s pricing framework would unduly and unreasonably limit access to life-saving and life-improving medicines for patients with rare

card_text: [ORG] Submission House of Commons Health Committee’s 2023 PMPRB Study Thank you for the opportunity to offer a rare disease innovator perspective to the committee’s current study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB). The [ORG] ([ORG]) is a network of 17 biopharmaceutical companies dedicated to improving the lives of patients with rare diseases by developing and commercializing treatments. During the past several years, [ORG] has actively engaged in the Health Canada and PMPRB consultation processes on both the regulations and the guidelines. [ORG] also has provided input on previous health committee studies regarding the PMPRB and rare disorders in previous parliaments. All its submissions are available on the [ORG] website (www.[ORG].ca). Background on Canadian rare disease treatment landscape Let’s begin by providing some background on the rare disease treatment landscape and some of the challenges faced by rare disease patients. Canadians affected by often severe and debilitating rare disorders already struggle to access needed treatments. This is in addition to the multiple other challenges faced by rare disease patients, including the very long journey to reach a diagnosis, small patient populations, fewer health care resources available to treat the conditions and many unknowns about the disease. For many rare disease patients, there are no medicines available yet to treat their condition. card_text: May 8, 2023 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Canada Subject: HESA study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB) Dear Honourable Members, As a not-for-profit organization that represents and promotes Ontario’s vibrant and diverse life sciences sector, [ORG] ([ORG]) has monitoried, engaged and researched the proposed changes to the PMPRB since amended regulations were first tabled in 2017. In the context of the House of Commons Health Committee (HESA) study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB), we are well positioned to shine light on the issue and the challenges that the Agency has presented for our members, the broader life sciences ecosystem and patients. As a science-based organization, [ORG] believes in evidence-based policymaking, and for this reason we have undertaken and supported research on the reforms over the past several years. Most recently, in 2022, [ORG] commissioned research from IQVIA – a global leader in health data and analytics – to examine new medicine launches in Canada to see whether and to what extent the new rules are having an impact. The data showed that the number of new drugs launched in Canada (i.e., not just submitted to Health Canada for approval but actually sold in Canada) declined steadily after the reforms were first conceived back in 2016, whereas global launches increased on average. From 2017-2021, there were an average of 34 annual new medicine launches globally but an average of just 20 per year in Canada.1 IQVIA

card_text: Office of the Chief Executive Officer Government of Canada House of Commons Submission Brief Study on Patented Medicine Prices Review Board [ORG] April 28, 2023 Submitted to Standing Committee on Health (HESA) The [ORG] (Neighbourhood Pharmacies) represents leading pharmacy organizations across the country, including chain, banner, long-term care, grocery chains, specialty pharmacies, and mass merchandisers with pharmacies across Canada. We advance the delivery of care through over 11,500 pharmacies and their teams, that serve as integral community health hubs in urban, suburban, rural, remote and First Nations neighbourhoods.1 As the association representing pharmacy operators across the country, we act as a panCanadian level to support policy makers with the development of innovative solutions that allow pharmacies to support primary care while advocating for fair and sustainable funding for community pharmacies. We are providing this submission to the Standing Committee on Health as part of its study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB), on behalf of our member pharmacies across the country. Specifically, we recommend a ‘whole of government approach’ to pharmaceutical pricing policy decisions that: • Recognizes the downstream impact of multiple pharmaceutical price compression policies on Canadians’ access to medications; • Reviews the mandate and oversight structures of the PMPRB to ensure that protecting Canadians from punitive medication prices does not result in loss of access to these medications; and • Involves broader stakeholder inclusion from across the entire patient care pathway in any further PMPRB initiatives. card_text: 28 April 2023 Submitted via email [CONTACT] CAPDM Submission to the Parliamentary HESA Committee re: PMPRB This submission is made on behalf of members of the Canadian Association for Pharmacy Distribution Management (CAPDM), in advance of the May 2, 2023 HESA Committee meeting to inform the study of the PMPRB. CAPDM is the national trade association for distributors that supply pharmacies and hospitals with over 95% of medicines consumed in Canada. Pharmaceutical distributors ensure the safe, secure, and timely access of prescription and over-the-counter medications to every corner of the nation, servicing over 12,500 pharmacies and hospitals. This efficient, accurate, and reliable supply chain ensures physical accessibility to medicines for all Canadians. CAPDM is supportive of the Committee’s decision to study the ongoing PMPRB reforms, which will have a significant unintended negative impact on core funding for Canada’s medication supply system. If these reforms are to proceed in their current format, they risk further destabilizing an already fragile drug supply system, making access to medications for Canadians, particularly those in rural and remote areas, more challenging. In light of the recent changes in leadership at the highest levels of PMPRB, we believe now is an opportune time to revisit the intentions of the proposed PMPRB reforms and hold an open discussion regarding the effects on the already-fragile drug supply and potential further weakening of infrastructure that enables national medication access. Price Compression and Impacts on Pharmaceutical Distribution CAPDM members strongly support medicine affordability, but not at the expense of accessibility.

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on the Treatment and Prevention of Cancer December 9, 2024 Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) represents more than 3 million workers, bringing together Canada’s national and international unions, along with provincial and territorial Federations of Labour and more than 100 district Labour Councils. Our members work in practically all sectors of the economy, in all occupations, in all parts of Canada. We are pleased to offer our position as it pertains to the treatment and prevention of cancer. Regardless of the approach taken by government, we submit that it should embody the determinants of health, and account for the realities and needs of equity-deserving groups, including women, racialized and Indigenous workers, and workers with disabilities. An Ounce of Prevention and the Right to Know The right of workers to know of hazards in their workplace is fundamental. The [ORG] has long taken the position that an “ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure” with respect to cancer, including those that are acquired on the job (occupational cancer). To this end, the fundamental right to know of hazards in the workplace must be adhered to, not only for known carcinogens, but also, employing the precautionary principle, for potential carcinogens. As such, all chemicals must be assessed utilizing a hazardsbased approach utilizing the hierarchy of controls; this means that, wherever possible, known or potentially carcinogenic chemicals must be avoided. card_text: [ORG] Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Parliament of Canada: Cancer Prevention Among Young People Submitted by: Marie Laberge, PhD Jena Webb, PhD Sami Fettah, PhD Sabrina Gravel, PhD Joseph-Omer Dyer, PhD Sylvain Letscher, PhD Nadielda Pastor-Bédard, MSc erg. Hélène Sultan-Taïeb, PhD France Labrèche, PhD December 3, 2024 Introduction We are a team of researchers associated with the Canada Research Chair in Adolescent Work Disability Prevention led by Marie Laberge. For the past two years, we have been conducting research intended to develop a program for early prevention of occupational cancers among adolescents with low qualifications and several vulnerabilities. We are submitting this brief to encourage the government to apply the precautionary principle when making decisions related to exposure to potentially toxic materials and environments. Both external factors and lack of experience make it more difficult to limit exposure among young people, and applying the precautionary principle would protect their health. Our objective is to prevent occupational cancers early on. It is estimated that between 4% and 8% of cancers are work-related,1 but this figure is probably an underestimate due to the difficulty in establishing causal links.2 In 2011 in Canada, roughly $4.4 billion in medical care costs, lost productivity and loss of quality of life3 are attributable to occupational cancers. card_text: Submission to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer November 2024 www.ac2orn.com Keith McIntosh Ac2orn ([ORG]), is pleased to provide the following comments to support the Committee’s study on the treatment and prevention of cancer in Canada. It is important for the Committee to allocate specific time to hear and consider the distinct issues faced by children and young people with cancer. Children are not small adults, and there are unique challenges to treating and conducting research on childhood, adolescent, and young adult cancers. When a young person is diagnosed with cancer it has profound long-term physical, emotional, and financial impacts on the individual and their family. Ac2orn was founded in 2014 to advocate for translational research and effective treatments to support the goal of curing childhood, adolescent, and young adult (CAYA) cancers. It is made up of parents from across Canada, who have children with different types of cancer, and the desire to help improve pediatric oncology research and outcomes. Ac2orn works to achieve its mission by connecting with CAYA health advocacy groups across Canada; identifying and addressing barriers that exist in accessing treatments and cancer clinical trials, and educating policy makers, industry, and others about the issues facing the CAYA cancer community. Name: November 1, 2024 To: House of Commons, Standing Committee on Health (HESA) via [CONTACT] From: [ORG]
card_text: November 1, 2024 To: House of Commons, Standing Committee on Health (HESA) via [CONTACT] From: [ORG] RE: The HESA study on Cancer Treatment and Prevention Subject: Urgent need for a dedicated HESA study reviewing Health Canada’s exposure limits for radiofrequency frequencies now used for 5G telecommunication and information technologies. Lack of evidence is not proof of safety. ---------------------- 5G wireless technologies such as smart phones, tablets, virtual headsets and wearables are being marketed and purchased across Canada, with enthusiasm. Millions of Canadians are being exposed to the non-ionizing radiofrequency electromagnetic fields that these devices emit in order to work. What most don’t realize is that the emissions from these technologies have not been safety-tested for health outcomes such as cancer. How do we know this? It is in a Health Canada 5G report that is available to the public upon request. In 2011, radiofrequency radiation, such as emitted by cell phones, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi and 5G technologies, was classified as a Group 2B, possible carcinogen, by the World Health Organization’s International Agency for Research on Cancer.1 IARC then noted particularly the need for studies in animals. In 2018, the U.S. National Toxicology Program concluded from its multimillion-dollar animal studies, 2 that cell phone type exposures, conducted under non-heating (non-thermal) conditions, caused cancer.3 Later in 2018, the Ramazzini Institute in Italy published similar findings for cell tower level exposures.4 This and subsequent evidence from human, animal and tissue studies, published in peer-reviewed journals, support reclassification to a Group 1 known human carcinogen,5

Name: Shelley Wright Director, [ORG] [CONTACT] Cancer Prevention
card_text: Shelley Wright Director, [ORG] [CONTACT] Cancer Prevention Submission to Parliament of Canada House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA), RF-EMR is an Environmental co-factor of Childhood Cancers Oct. 12, 2024 The national non-profit [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits, there is mounting evidence and concern that radiofrequency electromagnetic radiation (RF-EMR) represents a serious environmental contributor to cancer requiring recognition, rigorous independent assessment, and public health attention to encourage families to practice safe tech when using wireless (e.g., RF-EMR microwave frequency) technology. I’m a retired teacher and Director of [ORG]. We work with families of children, and teachers, injured by wireless RF-EMR. Children and school staff currently suffer chronic or debilitating adverse effects, including cancers, caused by continuous and cumulative RF-EMR in schools, playgrounds, and daycares. It is unnecessary to expose children to harmful radiation; safer wired technologies exist. Cancer prevention is far more effective and humane, and less costly for children, families, and society than treatment. While early detection has value, reduction of toxic exposures at homes and school environments should be paramount. We are deeply concerned that the escalating use of wireless devices and infrastructure (cell towers and antennas) intensifies RF-EMR exposures in educational settings. A substantial body of scientific evidence shows RF-EMR can cause biological effects linked to serious health issues, including cancers. Although the World Health Organization’s International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classified RF radiation as a possible carcinogen (Group 2B) in 2011, a cancer epidemiology update demonstrates a clearer link between electromagnetic radiation and cancer.1

card_text: Access to Gamma Knife in Canada Study: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer Brief to the Standing Committee on Health November 3, 2024 Ioana Grigorescu November 3, 2024 Brief to the Standing Committee on Health Study: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health, Thank you for conducting this study. This brief refers to decades of inequity in Canada regarding access to highly specialized brain radiation treatments. Depending on their postal code, Canadians are offered or denied access to specialized brain radiation. In 2002, Canadian Premiers agreed on equitable access to specialized treatments performed in centres of excellence. BC Standing Committee on Health issued the report “Patients First 2002: The Path to Reform” (1). It states: Sites of Excellence For High Risk - Low Volume Surgery In 2002, the Premiers of the provinces agreed to cooperate on consolidating high-risk, low volume surgery in a few designated centres across the country. The decision came from increasing research evidence, including the coroner inquest into the deaths of 12 children in Winnipeg’s pediatric cardiac surgery program, that large population densities are needed to safely deliver rare, high-risk surgery. Pediatric cardiac surgery, rarer organ transplants, and gamma knife radiation neurosurgery are examples of the advanced techniques that require specialized equipment and a highly experienced and trained team of health care providers. Name: Cancer treatment and prevention Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) September 13, 2024 - updated: October 8, 2024 [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits that primary can
card_text: Cancer treatment and prevention Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) September 13, 2024 - updated: October 8, 2024 [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits that primary cancer prevention, addressing root contributors to the disease process, merits the highest priority, to prevent cancer before it starts. Screening is secondary prevention, hopefully averting the worst with earlier treatment. We are scientists, medical experts and other volunteers, focusing diverse knowledge of environmental health, medicine, chemistry and scientific approaches on cancer. Here, we summarize why and how to “make least-toxic the norm” for cancer prevention. When [ORG] was established in 2007, heart disease was Canada’s #1 cause of death. Today, more Canadians die from cancer than any other single cause, and aggressive cancers are developing everearlier in life. Costs associated with screening, diagnoses, treatment and follow-up are unsustainable; lost wages are substantial; and patients’ and families’ pain and losses must not be under-estimated. Cancer trends We join many scientists stressing urgency to identify and address underlying cancer risk factors. While long-term Canadian data is lacking, we know that similar to American data, cancer diagnoses and deaths are increasing, particularly at younger ages.1,2 Cancer incidence is also increasing in younger Canadians,3 with the highest rates among White and Indigenous populations. • Liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancer, kidney cancer, melanoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma are increasing in younger Canadians.4 • Colorectal cancer has increased in those 20 to 44 years of age.5 Recently, with colorectal cancer rates increasing in both females and males

Name: [ORG]’s Recommendations to the Standing Committee on Health on the Study on Prevention and Treatment of Cancer Cancer remains the leading cause of death in Canada. An estimated 2 in 5 people living in
card_text: [ORG]’s Recommendations to the Standing Committee on Health on the Study on Prevention and Treatment of Cancer Cancer remains the leading cause of death in Canada. An estimated 2 in 5 people living in Canada will be diagnosed with cancer in their lifetime and about 1 in 4 will die from cancer. In 2024, 247,100 people living in Canada are estimated to be diagnosed.1 With an ageing population, the number of people diagnosed with cancer and cancer mortality is projected to rise significantly in the coming decades. While our health care system is pushed to the brink, people living with cancer continue to face barriers at every step of their cancer journey. The reality is people in Canada living with cancer are dying while they wait for care. Many are faced with the impossible choice of putting food on the table or taking on significant debt as they struggle to afford the out-of-pocket costs associated with a cancer diagnosis, such as, drugs and treatments, medical transportation and accommodation and loss of pay due to inability to work, among other costs. For underserved and equity-deserving populations in Canada, these challenges are even greater. CANA’s recommendations are intended to guide action that will promote better health and better outcomes for all people living with cancer in Canada. We urge the Standing Committee on Health to consider the issues presented here and incorporate our recommendations during your discussion on the study on the treatment and prevention of cancer. Name: Hypothesis on the Global Pandemic of the Early Onset of Cancers Michael Gilbertson, PhD Brief to the Parliament of Canada House of Commons Health Committee 3 September, 2024
card_text: Hypothesis on the Global Pandemic of the Early Onset of Cancers Michael Gilbertson, PhD Brief to the Parliament of Canada House of Commons Health Committee 3 September, 2024 I am Michael Gilbertson and I am an independent researcher. I have a Doctor of Philosophy degree in occupational and environmental health from the University of Stirling, in Scotland. In 2004, I retired after thirty-four years with the Government of Canada. In the 1970s, I was part of the organization charged with implementing the Environmental Contaminants Act (1975) and I worked as the Early Warning Coordinator identifying sentinel biological events from exposures to chemicals in the environment. My final years of service were with the International Joint Commission where I specialized in inferring the causal relations between the biological effects observed in fish, wildlife and human populations exposed to persistent toxic chemicals in Great Lakes food webs. Following my retirement from public service, I co-chaired the Halifax Project, an initiative involving 350 cancer scientists from 31 countries, half of whom were focused on the potential role of low-dose, biologically disruptive, common environmental chemicals that might be acting in concert with one another and contributing to various aspects of carcinogenesis. I was a co-author in this project and the results of the work were published in a series of articles in a special issue of Carcinogenesis, a journal of Oxford University Press, and which was also discussed in Environmental Health Perspectives, another peer reviewed journal with a high impact factor. org: Centre for Addiction and Mental Health
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Health Committee Study on Women’s Health Prioritizing Women’s Health Research Will Improve Health For All Submitted by Laura Gravelsins, Tallinn Splinter and Liisa Galea on behalf of Women’s Health Research Cluster and womenmindTM at the [ORG]’s April 29, 2024 Websites: https://www.galealab.com/ https://womenshealthresearch.ubc.ca/ https://www.camh.ca/en/get-involved/join-the-cause/womenmind Women’s health research has been undervalued, understudied, and underfunded for decades, which leads to disparities in health for everyone 1,2. Although human females live longer than human males, they are also more likely to live with chronic disease, and a recent world economic forum report suggested that females spend 25% more time across their lifetime in poorer health compared to males3. For many disorders, there are sex differences in prevalence, as women are twice more likely to be diagnosed with depression and Alzheimer’s disease than men4. Not only are sport concussions twice more likely to occur in women than in men, women show poorer outcomes than men5. Women have a unique physiology and experiences that impact their health differently compared to men, and many mental health conditions affect women differently and at different times in their lives than men6. Indeed, the time of greatest risk for first-time depression in a female’s lifetime is during the postpartum 7,8. And yet, despite these known differences we found that only 3% of over 3000 neuroscience and psychiatry studies considered women’s health variables questions 9. Name: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) 44th Parliament of Canada May 2024
card_text: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) 44th Parliament of Canada May 2024 Submited by: Dr. Neeru Gupta, PhD ([CONTACT]) Full Professor, Department of Sociology, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, NB Key consideration for the women’s health study: Ending the neglect of women’s health research involves ending the neglect of women in health workforce research Among the core strategic outcomes of the federal government of Canada is ensuring a health system that is responsive to the needs of Canadians. To help achieve this, many health agencies have adopted the Quadruple Aim framework for beter health care and equity: improving patient experiences, the health of populations, health care provider experiences, and value for money.1,2 According to the latest 2021 census data, women represent just over half (50.7%) of the Canadian population; women also represent four-fiŌhs (80.1%) of health care professionals.3 Yet, we still know remarkably litle Standing Commitee on Health, Addressing Canada’s health workforce crisis,5 which made no mention of women and no acknowledgement of the potential unintended consequences of gender-blind policies to recruit and retain health workers. Research is scarce on how factors salient to women drive health workforce outcomes. Despite the importance of women health care workers to a high-performing health system, data to support research and policy are siloed and incomplete, including in Canada. card_text: [CONTACT] – Unit 5 London ON [CONTACT] t. [CONTACT] f. [CONTACT] [CONTACT] u Providing counselling, advocacy, and support for abused women. Brief to the Standing Committee on Health Submitted by: Jennifer Dunn, Executive Director, London Abused Women’s Centre (Canada) April 15, 2024 Chair and Members, Standing Committee on Health: The London Abused Women’s Centre (LAWC) is a feminist organization that supports and advocates for personal, social, and systemic change directed at ending male violence against women and girls. LAWC is a non-residential agency that provides women and girls over the age of 12 who have been subjected to abuse, assault, sexual exploitation, trafficking, and non-state torture with immediate access to long-term, traumainformed counselling, advocacy, and support. In 2023, LAWC had over 11,000 service interactions. Of those 11,000, over 4,000 individual counselling, group and urgent support service interactions. This includes approximately 400 women and girls who reported being trafficked or were at risk of being trafficked. This also includes 112 women who reported being subjected to non-state torture. During this same time, LAWC also answered over 7,000 calls for support. It is impossible to study women’s health without looking through the lens of male violence against women and inequality. Despite strides toward ending male violence, women continue to navigate through societal structures that perpetuate their vulnerability. This brief explains the dimensions of women's health based on the work we do at the London Abused Women’s Centre which we are hoping will “Shine the Light”1 on the urgent need for comprehensive support

card_text: Improving Black Women’s Health in Canada Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health By Professor Bukola Salami, Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Black and Racialized People’s Health, Department of Community Health Sciences, University of Calgary Craig Wellington, Chief Executive Officer, Black Opportunity Fund Jeanne Lehman, Executive Director, Black Women in Action Dr. Modupe Tunde-Byass, Obstetrician and Gynecologist, North York General Hospital; Associate Professor, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, University of Toronto Paul Bailey, Executive Director, Black Health Alliance Professor Andre Renzaho, Distinguished Professor, Translational Health Research Institute, Western Sydney University Dr. Ato Sekyi-Otu, Orthopedic Surgeon, William Osler Health System; Lecturer, Department of Surgery, University of Toronto Mary Olukotun, PhD Student, Faculty of Nursing, University of Alberta Dr. Regine King, Associate Professor, Faculty of Social Work, University of Calgary Dr. Oluwakemi Amodu, Assistant Professor, Faculty of Nursing, University of Alberta Professor Solina Richter, Dean, College of Nursing, University of Saskatchewan Dr. Tehseen Ladha, Assistant Professor, Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry, University of Alberta Dr. Maria Beatriz Ospina, PhD, Associate Professor, Department of Public Health Sciences, Queen’s University April 2024 Black people are a growing population in Canada. Between 2016 and 2021, the Black population in Canada grew from 3.5% to 4.3% of the total Canadian population (Statistics Canada, 2023). As of 2021, Black people comprised 16.1% of the racialized population in Canada, with approximately 1.5 million people identifying as Black. Black women experience poorer economic and social outcomes than the general Canadian population.

Name: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by: Dr. Ryan Van Lieshout, MD, PhD, FRCPC ([CONTACT]) Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioural Neurosciences, M
card_text: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by: Dr. Ryan Van Lieshout, MD, PhD, FRCPC ([CONTACT]) Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioural Neurosciences, McMaster University Hamilton, ON The Perinatal Mental Health of Women, Pregnant Persons, Mothers and Birthing Parents in Canada: Perinatal mental health problems affect up to 1 in 5 mothers and birthing parents,1 rates that increased to 1 in 3 during the COVID-19 pandemic.2 Every case of postpartum depression (PPD) alone is associated with costs of up to $125,000 over the lifespan3 or $2.5B for each single year of births in Canada. The offspring of mothers with PPD are up to five times more likely to develop a clinically significant behavioural problem, and up to four times more likely to develop depression in their lives.4,5 Even though effective treatments can help both mothers and their children, as few as 1 in 10 pregnant and postpartum persons are able to access evidence-based care in Canada.6 Barriers to Care: There are many barriers to the receipt of timely perinatal mental health care in this country. One of the most significant is the current absence of structured and coordinated care pathways7 that identify sufferers and match the right person to the right treatment at the right time. Since most individuals with perinatal mental health problems will respond to psychotherapy and/or medications, another significant challenge is the limited knowledge possessed by front-line physicians about the relative safety of these medications during pregnancy and lactation. Our Grassroots Science began in 1993, in Nova Scotia. Laws, policies, and practices have nationally imposed discriminatory silencing on women’s mental health and their ability to openly address the impacts of non-State torture victimization in a manner that supports their human dignity, hope in their healability, and in being healed. card_text: Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Submitted on behalf of the [ORG] Contact: Helena Sonea [CONTACT] March 2024 cervical cancer screening in Canada, with limited harms in terms of lifetime risk of developing the cancer.7 [ORG] believes transitioning to primary HPV testing, with the option to self-collect, as the primary method of cervical cancer screening will increase screening uptake and improve the process and accuracy of screening results among people in Canada and reduce the burden of cervical cancer on our population in the long term. As provinces and territories prepare for this change, the swabs used for HPV tests have an indication that they must be done by a healthcare provider. Health Canada has an opportunity to update this indication, validate for laboratories what is needed for self-sampling or self-collection, and remove this barrier in providing at-home tests. [ORG] calls upon Health Canada to make this update promptly. Improve HPV vaccination rates About 75% of sexually active people contract an HPV infection at least once in their lifetime.8 Usually, HPV infections will clear on their own; though sometimes the infection persists. A high-risk HPV infection can lead to changes in the cells of the cervix, which can develop into cervical cancer if they are not found early. Getting vaccinated against HPV is the best way to eliminate the risk of HPV infection, as well as reduce the likelihood of several cancers. [ORG] recommends children and adults between the ages of 9 and 45 are vaccinated against HPV

card_text: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Breast cancer screening guideline and screening concepts from the [ORG] Submitted February 14, 2024, by: Dr. Guylène Thériault, Co- Chair, MD, CCFP Dr. Ahmed Abou-Setta, Co- Chair, MD, PhD Dr. Scott Klarenbach, Vice-Chair, MD, MSc, FRCPC Introduction In 2024, the [ORG] (Task Force) will release recommendations on screening for breast cancer to update our 2018 guideline (1). This is a priority for the Task Force and is important to Canadians, primary care providers, and other stakeholders. Since last summer, the Task Force has been working to provide updated recommendations based on a detailed, comprehensive review of available evidence using international best practices. This submission aims to provide information on the guideline update and general screening concepts. This guideline update is for women* at average and moderately above average risk of breast cancer and not for those with genetic risks or signs and symptoms of breast cancer (e.g. breast lump) (3). • To include the best available, current evidence, we have expanded our review to include different types of studies (randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and are conducting modelling). Research plans are publicly available (3). • Given the volume and complexity of the workload, the evidence review and modeling teams have requested more time to complete their tasks (1). card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Commi3ee on Health Study on Women’s Health Menopause Care in Canada Submi&ed by [ORG] / Société Canadienne de Ménopause (CMS/SCM) February 7, 2024 Impact of Menopause Symptoms The impact of menopause symptoms when not adequately managed is staggering. In a survey from the Menopause Foundation of Canada (MFC) in 2022 in Canada, one in two women felt unprepared for menopause and 4 in 10 women felt alone during this 7me. 3 The effect of symptoms on work productivity is being recognized worldwide, including in Canada, with a reported $3.5 billion per year proposed loss to the economy from lost productivity, missed days of work and lost income. 4 Up to 10% of women will quit their jobs because of severe symptoms that impact their ability to function. With midlife women at the prime of their work life, these numbers are very concerning. Furthermore, untreated moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms has been linked to negative outcomes later in life including cardiovascular disease. 5 Inadequate Care for Menopause in Canada With more than 10 million women in Canada currently at the age of perimenopause or post menopause the 7me is now to ensure there is adequate support for midlife women. Unfortunately, the level of menopause care available in Canada is inadequate and many women are not ge^ng the help they need. 3 Menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) is the most effective therapy to manage bothersome menopause symptoms. That number is anticipated to grow to more than 1.7 million by 2025. These people live with Alzheimer’s dementia, vascular dementia, young onset dementia, or one of over 50 different diseases or conditions that can cause dementia. Alzheimer disease and dementia are women's issues In high-income countries, for roughly every 1 male that is diagnosed with dementia, 2 females will be diagnosed. In 2020, an estimated 61.8% of persons living with dementia in Canada were female. By 2050, our study projects that over 1 million women will be living with dementia in Canada. Women are also doubly affected, as they provide the majority of care to people living with dementia in Canada. Statistics Canada found that in 2022, women who were unpaid caregivers for care-dependent adults spent an average of 10 hours a week providing care, compared with 6 hours for men. Also, the nature of dementia requires, on average, more hours of caregiving: the Canadian Institute for Health Information has found that “caregivers of seniors with dementia provide an average of 26 hours of care each week. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Women’s Cancers Date: February 12, 2024 Submitted by: Dr Shannon Salvador
org: The Society of Gynecologic Oncology of Canada
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Women’s Cancers Date: February 12, 2024 Submitted by: Dr Shannon Salvador President-Elect, Society of Gynecologic Oncology of Canada (GOC) Associate Professor, McGill University Head of Colposcopy, Jewish General Hospital Program Director, McGill Gynecologic Oncology Residency Program A history of gynecologic oncology and the Society of Gynecologic Oncologists of Canada (GOC) Historically, women’s cancers have been orphaned from traditional cancer care models. In the 1970s and 80’s a dedicated group of physicians created the gynecologic oncology sub-specialty to care for women with cancers of the ovary, uterus, cervix, vulva, and placenta. Among all surgical cancer specialties, ours is unique because the care is comprehensive, including diagnosis, surgery, systemic treatments (e.g., chemotherapy), surveillance, and palliative care. Only recently has patient care incorporated other disciplines, such as dedicated medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, palliative care teams and specialized nurses have begun collaborating with gynecologic oncology teams. Due to such historic isolation and separation from the traditional oncology care model, these cancers have long been deemed a “women’s issue.” Funding for clinical care and research has not kept pace with other, more common, cancers, such as colorectal, breast and lung. GOC, a non-profit organization, was created 40 years ago as a forum for medical professionals to highlight issues in women’s cancer care across Canada and help correct the disparity in cancer care access and equity in research funding for new treatments1. The vision of GOC is to be the voice of gynecologic cancer

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Transforming Canada’s Gynecologic Cancer Care: Evidence-Based, Equitable Access for All February 6, 2024 Submitted by: Gillian Hanley and Jessica McAlpine Ariadne Holness de Hiller David Huntsman Nancy McKinstry CM Gavin Stuart Michelle Woo Please address any correspondence to: [CONTACT] and [CONTACT] Over half of Canada’s population is at risk of developing a gynecological cancer over their lifetime. Gynecologic cancer is any cancer that starts in the reproductive organs. The five types of gynecologic cancer are: cervical, ovarian, uterine, vaginal and vulvar. Gynecological cancer has significant socioeconomic impact, both in direct costs [e.g, in 2021, $781M direct health system costs] and incalculable indirect costs related to loss of productivity and societal impacts of the disease.(1) Clearly, improved prevention, diagnosis and treatment, and survivorship, are key to mitigating the burden and impact of gynecological cancers. In Canada, there are profound disparities in cancer care delivery within and between provinces.(1) Differences between provincial healthcare policies means that a woman affected by gynecologic cancer may receive dramatically different care in Alberta, Ontario, or Newfoundland. The disparities are greater in areas where access to, or receipt of, cancer care is compromised due to geographic, cultural, political or socio- economic factors. Equitable, culturally-appropriate, trauma-informed delivery of care remains a significant challenge. In 2019, in British Columbia, we created the Gynecologic Cancer Initiative. card_text: @itsourtimecanada Dr. Amy Louis-Bayliss Dr. Woganee Filate Dr. Sarah Naomi Shaw Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study January 14, [CONTACT]. Amy Louis-Bayliss, BHSc, MD, FRCPC Emergency Medicine and Anesthesia Mississauga, ON Dr. Woganee Filate MHSc, MD, FRCPC Respirology and Sleep Medicine Toronto Sleep and Pulmonary Centre, Toronto Ontario Dr. Sarah Naomi Shaw, MSW, EdD, MD, CCFP, FCFP Family Physician, Taddle Creek Family Health Team Chair, Board of Directors, Taddle Creek Family Health Team Lecturer, University of Toronto, Dept of Family and Community Medicine Background We are three Canadian-trained physicians with over forty years combined practice experience in primary care, emergency medicine and internal medicine. We are committed to improving access to menopause care for Canadian women. Menopause is the permanent cessation of ovarian function, and this clinically presents as no menstruation for twelve months. Menopause is a turning point in a woman’s life where her risks of osteoporosis, uterine prolapse, recurrent bladder infections, cardiovascular disease, and dementia increase significantly. Even though menopause is a universal stage of life for all women, there are few opportunities for training and physicians often feel inadequately prepared to offer menopause care even as more female patients are demanding it. Providing a comprehensive assessment at menopause is paramount to healthy aging for women and a critical juncture to prevent chronic disease. We are advocating that Canada must establish universal menopause assessments as standard of care. On behalf of our patients and women across this country, we look forward

Name: SUBMISSION TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY January 25, 2024
card_text: SUBMISSION TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY January 25, 2024 SUBMITTED BY [ORG] Please address any correspondence to: Tania Vrionis, Chief Executive Officer [CONTACT] [CONTACT] INTRODUCTION Ovarian cancer is the most fatal women’s cancer, with a five-year survival rate of only 44%.1 This means that of the eight women a day who are diagnosed with ovarian cancer in Canada only four will survive five years. There is no screening test for ovarian cancer and no vaccine to prevent it. Symptoms are non- specific (e.g., bloating, abdominal pain/discomfort, urinary symptoms, changes in bowel habits) and diagnostic pathways and tools are limited making timely diagnosis extremely challenging. Ovarian cancer is not one disease. In fact, it is several different diseases making the path to scientific discoveries and improved treatments particularly complex. With very few exceptions, available treatments have not changed significantly since the 1990s. For the 3,100 Canadians diagnosed with ovarian cancer this year, they will be treated with the same methods, surgery and chemotherapy, and unfortunately these methods are not successful in most patients. Until recently, breast cancer research in Canada has received six times and prostate cancer research over two times the funding that has been invested in ovarian cancer research, despite a comparatively worse prognosis.2 It is not surprising that outcomes for ovarian cancer have not improved much in 50 years. Effective and strategic research investment is a core lever for change and a focus of this submission, but other opportunities for improving

Name: Submission House of Commons Standing Committee on Health
card_text: Submission House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study The Impacts of Canada’s Breast Screening Guidelines: One Patient’s Experience Carolyn Holland, BRM My name is Carolyn Holland and I was diagnosed with Stage IIB synchronous bilateral breast cancer at age 43. I am writing with the hopes that my experience can save other Canadian women from the travesty that is the Canadian breast screening guidelines. One night in 2021, I found a lump in each breast in the shower—I had never been told about mammography in the 40s by my family doctor and self-exam was never suggested or encouraged. To add insult to injury, I had to wait more than three months for a mammogram and ultrasound following my discovery of the two lumps. After that, however, things moved quickly. Within the span of the subsequent month, I was given additional imaging, underwent multiple biopsies, and received the diagnosis. Although I knew based on my reports it wasn’t good, I still wasn’t prepared to actually hear the words from my breast surgeon. A month later, I had my first chemotherapy session, five days before Christmas. I underwent seven more rounds of chemo. For rounds 5 and 6, I was given Paclitaxel but I had severe allergic reactions both times and so my oncologist switched me to Taxotere. I now have neuropathy and permanent chemotherapyinduced alopecia. I am also at risk of a life-threatening allergic reaction if I am exposed to the same solvents that made Paclitaxel a problem

Name: WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Impact of the Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines on my Diagnosis
card_text: WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Impact of the Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines on my Diagnosis Annie Slight, B.A. Psychoeducation I had my first mammogram at age 40. I didn’t have a family history of breast cancer, I was young and healthy, so when the results came back normal, I was relieved, and just moved on. A few months later, I felt a small lump in my right breast. I didn’t worry too much-I had always had lumpy breasts-but still scheduled an appointment with my gynecologist. Following a physical exam, he said it was one of my normal glands, noting that my recent mammogram had come back normal so I shouldn’t worry. I trusted him and trusted my mammogram. The mass was growing, so I went back to see my doctor, who again reassured me, but finally sent me for an ultrasound. I remember that day like it was yesterday. The radiologist asked me to stay for a biopsy. I had one done that day and a few days later, I was diagnosed with stage 2, grade 3 breast cancer, over a year after feeling the small lump. I was 42. It’s only after my diagnosis that I started hearing about my breast density; I was informed that I had dense breasts, which increased my risk of developing breast cancer. I was told that looking for cancer on my mammogram was like looking for a polar bear in a snowstorm, as dense tissue appears white

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Health Committee Study on Women’s Health [ORG] January 23, 2024 Fertility Issues in Canada [ORG] ([ORG]) appreciates the opportunity to contribute to the Women's Health study conducted by the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health. As the leading national organization providing free support, public education, and advocacy for equitable access to fertility care in Canada, our submission underscores the critical need for funding in fertility treatments and reproductive assistance. While this addresses the challenges faced by Canadian women struggling with fertility, it is also applicable to men. By taking action on this front, we can provide much-needed support to those seeking to have children and ensure equitable access to vital fertility care across the country. The need for fertility care in Canada becomes evident when considering: • Canada's fertility rate has undergone a decline of 15% since 2010, signifying a pressing need to address the challenges faced by individuals and couples trying to conceive. • One in six Canadians experience fertility struggles at some point in their life, underscoring the prevalence and significance of this issue. • The cost of fertility care, ranging from approximately $10,000 to $20,000 per cycle of in vitro fertilization (IVF), is a substantial financial burden, and achieving successful outcomes often requires multiple cycles. • Beyond the medical perspective of infertility, members of the 2SLGBTQI+ community and single individuals also seek access to fertility care to fulfill their dreams of having children, further highlighting the diverse range of people

Name: Women's Health Study House of Commons Standing Commitee on Health Beyond the Breast Screening Guidelines: A
card_text: Women's Health Study House of Commons Standing Commitee on Health Beyond the Breast Screening Guidelines: A Personal Account and Call for Change Jennifer Borgfjord, B.P.E., B. Ed., M. Ed. My name is Jennifer Borgfjord. I'm writing to the Standing Commitee on Health to share my cancer experience with healthcare decision-makers to call for change in breast screening guidelines in Canada. My breast cancer story is one of dense breasts and possibly a tum our developing in my late 40s. Up until May 19, 2023, I was considered not to have an increased risk for breast cancer, according to the Canadian Task Force Guidelines; no family history, no genetic mutations, and no previous chest radiation. The only subtle indication was that throughout a series of medical appointments over several decades, I was told I had "lumpy" breasts. My first mammogram letter reported a confusingto-me statistic on my breast density, but the letter for my next mammogram made it clear about my breast density, the risk factors, and that mammograms do not detect all cancers. My most recent mammogram, in March 2023, and probably the one before it, in September 2020, did not pick up a malignant ~2 cm tumors or the Ductal Carcinoma in Situ later noted in my surgical pathology report. On April 24, 2023, I found the lump by self-checking one month after I received a letter from the screening program saying my results were "normal." A very long fifteen days unfolded. card_text: Women's Health Study House of Commons Standing Committee on Health How Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines Impacted my Diagnosis Gaynor Hart My name is Gaynor Hart. I’m a wife and mother of two young daughters, now 11 and 13, living in Nova Scotia. I work full time as a corporate clerk in a busy law firm in downtown Dartmouth. I'm writing to the Standing Committee on Health to share my story regarding the breast cancer screening guidelines and how they negatively impacted our life. Fortunately, in Nova Scotia women can self-refer to start mammograms at age 40. Sadly, my mother passed away when I was 24 of cancer of the maxillary sinus which metastasized to her brain so I had seen the horrors of cancer, and treatment, at a young age. This has always led me to be extremely pro-active with anything that can diagnose cancer at an early stage. I signed up for the Nova Scotia Breast Screening Program and had annual mammograms from my early 40's without fail. In 2018, just before my scheduled mammogram in June, I noticed a lump underneath my right breast. I was 47 years old. I told the radiographer and she marked the area with a triangular sticker. She said it would show on the mammogram and notify the doctor reviewing it to pay extra attention to that area. Name: Submission To the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study
card_text: Submission To the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study The Harmful Effects of Current Breast Cancer Screening Recommendations Submitted In Memory of My Sister Sharon Olson By Marj Olson Women need breast cancer screening starting at age 40 and they need to be carefully informed of the risks caused by breast density. In addition, supplemental screening for women with dense breasts is crucial to ensure that cancer isn't being hidden by the dense breast tissue as it was in my sister’s case. Currently, the Canadian guideline recommends against supplemental screening for women with dense breasts. Our family has seen the tragic results. Sharon Olson was born May 10, 1962 and died August 5, 2022. She was Lois and Roland Olson’s fifth of seven children. After being diagnosed with breast cancer in November of 2017, Sharon thought back to why she hadn’t gone for regular screening mammograms. As a teacher, it was difficult to find time during business hours to call and make an appointment and then drive into the city to go to the appointment. Since her mammograms were “grossly unremarkable” and because we didn’t have a close family tie to breast cancer, it did not seem to be worth the effort to continue. When Sharon found a lump in her armpit her doctor sent her for a mammogram which found nothing due to the extreme density of her breasts, but the subsequent ultrasound found a large tumor. Chiropractors are one of Canada’s experts in the assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of musculoskeletal (MSK) conditions, such as back pain, headaches, arm or neck strain and diseases of the muscle and joints, as well as the management of pain caused by these conditions. Each year, more than 11 million Canadians suffer from musculoskeletal conditions and by 2031 this number is projected to grow to an alarming 15 million.1 MSK conditions are having a devastating impact on the health, quality of life, and workforce participation of Canadians, as well as on Canada’s economy. Name: Women’s Health Study House of Commons Standing Committee on Health How Canadian Breast Screening
card_text: Women’s Health Study House of Commons Standing Committee on Health How Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines Impacted My Diagnosis Sherry Wilcox, Hon. B.A., LL.B. My name is Sherry Wilcox. I’m writing to the Standing Committee on Health to share my story because I do not want other Canadians to go through what I went through. I recall asking about a mammogram when I turned 40 and was told by my family doctor that I was too young, that my breasts were too dense, and that screening didn’t start until I turned 50 anyways. From my experience and those of my contacts, the rhetoric espoused by the Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care around shared decision-making does not reflect what’s actually happening in doctor’s offices. I was not asked about my values and preferences. I was simply denied a mammogram. At the age of 44, I felt a small lump in my right breast very close to my underarm. My physician examined me and suggested it was likely a benign fibroadenoma. That was reassuring, but I requested a mammogram and was told the same thing that I was when I was 40: that I was too young, that my breasts would be too dense and, consequently, that they likely wouldn’t see anything anyways. After pushing a bit more, my doctor agreed to send me for an ultrasound. After the ultrasound, my physician called me to tell me that there was something slightly suspicious but that I should go for another ultrasound

card_text: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Dr. Kathleen Ross, President Canadian Medical Association Introduction Since 1867, the Canadian Medical Association (CMA) has advocated for physicians and championed the health of Canadians. The CMA brings a unique perspective on women’s health, with a focus on the women physicians who care for Canadians. Their health holds significance beyond the way it affects their own well‐being. As Canada’s health care is being provided increasingly by women physicians, the wellness of this demographic is becoming of greater and greater importance to the larger public. It is getting harder to ignore a central truth — the health of our women physicians is suffering. Women physicians There are myriad systemic issues that continue to negatively influence the ability of women physicians to flourish under the current conditions of their work life. More and more commonly, women physicians are experiencing worsening wellness outcomes, with significantly higher rates of burnout and screening positive for depression. According to the CMA’s National Physician Health Survey results (2021), burnout among women physicians is a shocking 16% higher than among their male counterparts.1 The pre‐ pandemic prevalence of suicidal ideation was significantly higher among women in medicine than among men and has remained high since 2021. Workload accounts for some of the imbalance in burnout. Administrative tasks make up a larger portion of women physicians’ weeks. card_text: December 14, 2023 To: The Standing Committee on Health: Women’s Health Topic: Reproductive Justice for Women and Gender-Diverse Im/migrants Submitted by: [ORG] ([ORG]) Reproductive Justice for Women and Gender-Diverse Im/migrants Due to the landscape of Canadian migration programs creating precarious statuses, public health care access is limited for precarious status migrants, with only agricultural workers, other workers with permits that often need to be over 12 months (in several provinces; Ibrahim, 2023), and refugee claimants (via the Interim Federal Health Program) eligible for public coverage. All others rely on private insurance, which is costly and not comprehensive, or go without coverage. We agree with the public statement by The Government of Canada that the “goal of ensuring that women and girls in all their diversity [emphasis added] can decide what to do with their bodies, their lives and their futures-without question” should be fully supported (Government of Canada, n.d.). We recognize that many of the barriers to reproductive justice are due to a legacy of systemic oppression that has subjugated racialized women and gender-diverse people. We call for more coordinated, systematic and sustained efforts to ensure accessibility to reproductive justice is broadened for women and gender diverse people with precarious immigrant status so that people of all diverse identities truly can decide what to do with their bodies. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study December 15, 2023 Submitted by: Janet Ko, President and Co-Founder The [ORG] Perimenopause is the time leading up to menopause and can last anywhere from six to eight years. During perimenopause estrogen and progesterone levels fluctuate – sometimes dramatically – leading to many symptoms that can have a negative impact on health and quality of life. Most women are in perimenopause between the ages of 40 and 50. Women are postmenopausal for the rest of their lives. Women experience symptoms for years, sometimes decades, and yet, they are not supported by the health system, their employers, or society as a whole. Menopause: a women’s health issue There are over 30 symptoms associated with perimenopause and menopause, including but not limited to lack of energy, depression, flushing and night sweats, muscle and joint ache, insomnia, bladder control issues, heart palpitations and headaches. There are too many to be included in a brief. Canadian women typically have a low awareness of the symptoms associated with menopause/perimenopauseiv. Every day millions of women in Canada are suffering through menopause without understanding what is happening to their bodies and where to turn for help. Beyond the symptoms, once a woman has reached menopause she is at greater risk for osteoporosis, heart disease and genitourinary issues. Leading up to and throughout this period, it is essential that middle-aged women have access to primary care providers who can support them in understanding and managing

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Urgent Need for Universal Coverage of Prescription Contraception Submitted by the [ORG] Please address any correspondence related to this brief to Heather Olszewski at [CONTACT] Purpose This brief describes the urgent need for universal coverage of prescription contraception in Canada. With approximately 40% of pregnancies in Canada being unplanned1, there is a significant and measurable cost burden to the health care systems that is quite preventable. Introduction As a national professional organization of experts in women’s health, The Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada ([ORG]) believes that access to contraception is essential for gender equity. Contraception allows women to have a reproductive life plan and to time their pregnancies. This in turn means that they are more likely to complete their educational goals, participate more fully in the workforce, enjoy more economic stability which benefits other children in the home, and have healthy pregnancies and carry to term when they do choose to have children. Financial barriers can limit the choice of contraception, leaving individuals to choose the cheapest method - not necessarily the most effective - or to use no method of contraception at all. What does this cost the health care system? Most unintended pregnancies do not result in abortion; approximately 57% will result in a birth1. The direct cost of unintended pregnancies in Canada is $320 million per year2. This analysis does not take into consideration any downstream costs to the individual and society. The

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Women’s Health December 2023 www.womensrightsmatter.ca
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Women’s Health December 2023 www.womensrightsmatter.ca | [CONTACT] This brief uses the term “woman” to mean adult human female and “man” to mean adult human male, while gender non-conforming male (“GNC males”) refers to males who identify as transwomen or non-binary, and “gender” is the societal expectations based on an individual’s sex. Introduction It was traditionally believed women’s health mostly mimicked men’s health. Along with the view that using women as research subjects is problematic because of fluctuation from menstrual cycles, resulted in a limited number of females as research subjects. This has caused a major disparity between men’s and women’s medical research. Heart attacks, for instance, present differently in women than men. While there has been knowledge about male heart health since the early 1900s, it wasn’t evident until the late 2000s that heart attacks present differently in women. To this day, just 1 in 3 research subjects are women (Heart and Stroke Foundation 2018, p.4). Recent research has shown that treatments that work for men can detrimentally impact women. In other fields experiments have sometimes been carried out without considering sex in scientific research. Scientists have often used only one sex (generally male) for experiments and applied the findings to both sexes, without solid grounds. These kinds of inadvertent extrapolations might cause unintentionally harmful results to the neglected sex and economic loss (Lee, p. 167). A 2009 survey analyzed sex bias in journal articles published in 10

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Consultations on Women’s Health Heart & Stroke November 30, 2023 Background Canada’s healthcare system is faced with a continued lack of awareness and understanding around women’s heart and brain health. Two-thirds of participants in clinical trials on heart disease and stroke have historically been men, and when women are included, sex and genderbased analysis and reporting (SGBAR) is not always conducted or included in reported results. Half of women who experience heart attacks have symptoms go unrecognized, and they are less likely than men to receive the treatments and medications they need or receive them in a timely manner. Women represent more than 50% of our population; it is time this massive gap is adequately addressed. Women are key contributors to societies and our economy; yet endure hardships that can lead to chronic disease risk and even to cardiac arrest, partly due to their important supportive role to society that they play. Efforts to improve and safeguard women’s health will contribute to a more resilient economy and society. At Heart & Stroke (H&S), we strive to live in a world where all women receive the care they need when it comes to heart and brain health. This means that women receive health information, diagnoses and care that considers their distinct risk factors related to gender and sex, risk factors that impact women differently, as well as intersecting and overlapping factors that add to their risk such as race, ethnicity, Indigeneity, socioeconomic status, sexual

card_text: 1 Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health November 30th, 2023 1 [ORG] works to promote health, well-being, and rights related to sexuality and reproduction. We provide direct support, referrals, and information to the public and work with decision-makers to advance progressive policies on access to abortion, stigma-free healthcare, gender equality, 2SLGBTQ+ rights, and inclusive sex-ed. Our vision is for all people everywhere to have full control over, and to decide freely upon, all matters related to their sexuality, reproduction, and gender, including their reproductive and sexual health. For more information, contact Insiya Mankani, Public Affairs Office ([CONTACT]) or Kelly Bowden, Director of Policy and Advocacy ([CONTACT]) Introduction Health as defined by the World Health Organization is not only the absence of disease or infirmity, but the complete physical, mental, and social well-being of each person. The WHO states that “the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health is one of the fundamental rights of every human being without distinction of race, religion, political belief, economic or social condition”. In short – a woman’s right to health is equal to all others, and her health is defined by her whole of being, not strictly the management of diseases. Despite this, significant gaps remain for women across our country to fulfill their right to health. Specifically addressing the unmet needs of women’s sexual and reproductive health is both crucial for addressing the individual needs of women and to addressing wider social goals such as gender equality.

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Endometriosis in Canada
org: Canadian Society for the Advancement of Gynecologic Excellence / EndoAct Canada / The Endometriosis Network Canada
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Endometriosis in Canada Submitted by Kate Wahl, Katie Luciani, and Liane Belland Questions concerning the content of this brief should be directed to: [CONTACT] [CONTACT] [CONTACT] Purpose This submission describes the challenges facing women, transgender, non-binary, and twospirit people living with endometriosis in Canada. It also highlights opportunities for the federal government to support the more than 1 million Canadians who have this disease by leading coordinated action to ensure timely diagnosis and management. Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 Canadian women Endometriosis impacts at least one in ten women and unmeasured numbers of transgender, non-binary, and two-spirit individuals. It is a complex and debilitating chronic disease that occurs when tissue that is like the lining of the uterus implants outside of the uterus to form lesions, cysts, and deep nodules. These growths are inflammatory and can be invasive, resulting in severe distortion of pelvic anatomy and infiltration of the abdominal cavity. Although endometriosis is commonly thought of as a gynecological disease, growths are often found on non-gynecological structures such as bowel, ureters, bladder, diaphragm, and even lymph nodes and the lungs.1 Left unmanaged, invasive endometriosis can result in life-threatening complications including bowel obstruction and organ death. There is no definitive cause or a known cure for endometriosis. Symptoms of endometriosis often begin in adolescence and include, but are not limited to, severe menstrual pain, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility as well as whole-body symptoms like fatigue.

Name: Submission to The House of Commons’ Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Endometriosis and Gynecological conditions
card_text: Submission to The House of Commons’ Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Endometriosis and Gynecological conditions Date: November 29th 2023 Submission by: Dr. Andrew Zakhari Dr. Dong Bach Nguyen Representing: MUHC EndoCARES [ORG] – Endometriosis Centre for the Advancement of Research and Surgery What is already known Endometriosis affects one in ten women and untold numbers of gender diverse people. It can cause debilitating pain, infertility, and significant emotional and psychological distress. This condition is characterized by an abnormal growth of highly inflammatory tissue within the abdomen or pelvis (similar to the inner lining of the uterus) that causes scarring, and can invade into other organs like the bowel or bladder. Less commonly, it may even affect pelvic nerves (like the sciatic nerve) or more remote areas such as the lungs or diaphragm. Endometriosis can be severe enough to cause loss of kidney function, to cause blockage of intestines, to produce large, inflammatory cysts in the pelvis, and much more. We know there is a lengthy time to first seeing a specialist, let alone obtaining a diagnosis, which may take up to a decade.1 Endometriosis is largely considered a benign condition, but we also know there is an increased risk of ovarian and peritoneal cancers in these patients, although this is poorly understood. Absenteeism from work or school and poor quality of life are sadly quite commonplace for patients with endometriosis, and there is a huge economic burden associated with it.2-4 Lastly, treating endometriosis correctly is challenging

Yaffe PhD, C.M., FRSC Professor, Department of Medical Biophysics, University of Toronto Senior Scientist, Physical Sciences Platform, Sunnybrook Research Institute Co-Director, Imaging Research Program Ontario Institute for Cancer Research Anna N Wilkinson, MSc, MD, CCFP, FCFP Associate Professor, University of Ottawa Jennie Dale, MBA, Cofounder and Executive Director, Dense Breasts Canada Why is breast cancer screening important? One in eight women will develop breast cancer. In 2022, 28,600 Canadian women were diagnosed with breast cancer and 5,500 died from it [1]. Early detection by screening helps to decrease the likelihood of death. Screening has been shown to decrease breast cancer mortality in Canada by 40% overall and 44% in women aged 40-49[2]. Living in a province that includes screening for women in their 40s is associated with a 1.9% increase in 10-year net survival [3]. This is a similar benefit to tamoxifen, a drug used for patients with hormone positive breast cancer. Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Women’s Health My Journey with Endometriosis Submitted by Maggie Archibald
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Women’s Health My Journey with Endometriosis Submitted by Maggie Archibald Dear Health Committee, Firstly, thank you for the opportunity to submit a brief. I am writing to bring attention to a health issue that affects millions of individuals, including myself, and to advocate for increased awareness, support, and resources for those impacted by endometriosis in Canada. As somebody with endometriosis, I have experienced firsthand the impact this chronic condition can have on individuals' lives. The debilitating pain, menstrual irregularities, and other associated symptoms significantly affect my daily activities and overall well-being. Endometriosis is a gynecological condition that affects approximately 1 million people in Canada -- 1 in 10 women and unmeasured numbers of transgender and gender-nonconforming people1. In spring 2022, I began attending the IWK’s Endometriosis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Clinic, the first of its kind in Atlantic Canada. Over the next eight months, I tried various treatments for my endometriosis symptoms until I concluded that surgery was the best option. In February 2023, I underwent surgery, which confirmed a diagnosis of endometriosis. My journey to a diagnosis of endometriosis took about 16 years. However, individuals typically spend seven to nine years discussing symptoms and pushing for answers until they get diagnosed. There is currently no known cure for endometriosis. While various treatments and interventions exist to manage symptoms and improve quality of life, none offer a cure. Living with endometriosis is an ongoing battle—navigating through pain, uncertainty, and societal misconceptions.

Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study November 2023
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study November 2023 The [ORG] ([ORG]) welcomes the opportunity to submit to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of Women’s Health. We would like to acknowledge that the Standing Committee on Health is comprised of 11 male members out of a total of 12. Despite efforts to enhance female representation through seat exchanges among members, it is disconcerting that the committee does not align with the gender parity goals of our government. We strongly encourage Members of Parliament across all political affiliations to prioritize gender parity in the selection of committee members, thus preventing any backslide in our collective efforts. Summary of Recommendations: 1. Address the long-standing health inequities faced by marginalized and vulnerable women. 2. Collaborate with provincial and territorial governments in creating a strategy to improve senior women’s health in Canada. 3. Establish protected policies and provisions that will not only support victims of gender-based violence but also prevent those from becoming victims in the first place. 4. Collaborate with provinces and territories in ensuring equitable access to abortion care. 5. Protect the interest and rights of LGBTQ+ Canadians by working with provincial and territorial governments to improve access to gender affirming care. Marginalized and Vulnerable Women The [ORG] calls on the federal government to address the long-standing health inequities faced by marginalized and vulnerable women.
