Name: Pickering, Ontario November 19, 2023 Honourable Members of the Standing Committee on Health: Women’s Health Long Term Effects of Adverse Childhood Experiences on Women's Health Summary
card_text: Pickering, Ontario November 19, 2023 Honourable Members of the Standing Committee on Health: Women’s Health Long Term Effects of Adverse Childhood Experiences on Women's Health Summary Women have more complex patterns of adverse childhood experiences; this has implications for their mental health and socioeconomic outcomes in adulthood. Women growing up in a dysfunctional home environment have a significant risk factor for depression, substance abuse, and intimate partner violence, which heightens their vulnerability to commercial sexual exploitation. In addition, women with cumulative ACE's experiences have elevated levels of toxic stress that will affect genetically their offspring and the quality of parental care. Mothers are the primary caregivers that newborns and toddlers rely on; for healthy development, they need a caregiver that is sensitive and responsive to their needs. Caregiver trauma impacts children’s psychological development. With target legislation, we can improve women’s health. Introduction [ORG] is a non-profit organization driven to fight the Commercial Sexual Exploitation of Women and Children through insightful research and assertive advocacy, thereby resulting in greater protection for women and children. [ORG] was founded by Michelle Abel. The “Status quo” Currently we do not know the economic burden of Adverse Childhood Experiences on the Canadian economy. The current numbers we find estimate that ACEs-related health consequences cost an estimated $748 billion annually in North America. Name: Addressing Gendered Islamophobia in Canadian Healthcare for Women Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study
card_text: Addressing Gendered Islamophobia in Canadian Healthcare for Women Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study November 17, 2023 dignity, stereotypical comments, prejudiced attitudes, and aggressive behaviour are only some of the experiences of Muslim patients at different healthcare settings. Studies describe instances where discrimination may have led to patients receiving suboptimal care and services from healthcare providers. Participants felt that discrimination led to them getting late diagnoses, being considered for discharge early, and not being taken seriously when describing symptoms and pain levels. Muslim patients also felt they were not offered services due to the personal prejudices of their healthcare providers. The scoping review also demonstrates that Muslims receive culturally inappropriate care due to health care staff’s lack of awareness of Islamic practices, such as the need for prayer spaces, modesty and privacy for patients, halal food options, and hygienic services. While this is not always considered a form of discrimination, patients describe having negative healthcare experiences because of it.2 One study from Newfoundland that looked at maternity health needs of immigrant Muslim women found that women experienced insensitivity and lack of knowledge about their religious and cultural practices. Name: First, before I get into it, I have to say: It's shocking you don't have more women on your committee. I'm a 39-year-old white cis woman from Montreal, Quebec currently living in the capital region. I
card_text: First, before I get into it, I have to say: It's shocking you don't have more women on your committee. I'm a 39-year-old white cis woman from Montreal, Quebec currently living in the capital region. I got my period when I was 11. After 24 years of persistently trying to get answers, I was diagnosed at the age of 35 with stage-four endometriosis and adenomyosis, with painful lesions on my uterus, ovaries, Fallopian tubes, cervix and bowel. I had a radical hysterectomy, which plunged me into sudden surgical menopause. Roughly one in 10 people assigned female at birth have endometriosis. It has been found in fetuses, premenstrual girls and postmenopausal people. It is a condition defined by the presence of endometriallike tissue outside of the uterus, creating lesions most commonly in the pelvic and abdominal region, and in some cases also in the lungs, brain, bladder, skin and other organs. There is no cure, and no scientific consensus on what causes it — not because it is exceptionally cryptic, but because funding is scant and medical researchers are not encouraged to study it. Currently, the way most people are treated is based on a 1920s definition of the disease that has been debunked in other parts of the world. Endometriosis is the leading cause of female infertility and costs Canadian society billions per year in healthcare costs, lost productivity, poor career prospects for sufferers and out-of-pocket paramedical and medical expenses. Name: ISSUE
card_text: ISSUE Contraception is not easily accessible through the province of Alberta. Financial and gatekeeping barriers must be resolved in order to provide comprehensive reproductive care for Albertans. PURPOSE To equitably provide free contraception to all Canadians for their medical needs and reproductive rights. RECOMMENDATION Health Canada address the gap in contraception access in Alberta by funding all forms of contraception including hormonal contraceptives, intrauterine devices (IUDs), condoms and emergency contraception pills. Pharmacies, schools, and government agencies should distribute these medications/products at no cost to improve access–especially to vulnerable populations. Health Canada should also fund additional resources for nurses in schools, prisons, shelters and non-governmental organizations (NGOs). RATIONALE The Canada Health Act requires publicly funded medically necessary services be provided, yet many in Canada either cannot access or cannot afford appropriate contraception (Chaudhary, 2021). Access is not equitable in every province, with British Columbia as the only province providing universal provision of contraception for their whole population (Global Contraception Policy Atlas, 2023). As with all good public health programs, expanding support for sexual and reproductive health saves lives and resources; comparable programs actually save government spending in a variety of areas as well as support the overall health of the population. The cost savings are estimated as: o The cost of delivering universal contraception across Canada would be $157 million. card_text: Healthcare Access Experiences and Needs of Lesbian, Bisexual, and Queer Women and of Trans, Nonbinary, and Intersex People in Canada: A Submission to the Federal Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study October 13, 2023 Summary Egale is Canada’s leading organization for 2SLGBTQI people and issues. We improve and save lives through research, education, awareness, and by advocating for human rights and equality in Canada and around the world. Our work helps create societies and systems that reflect the universal truth that all persons are equal and none is other. In this brief, we offer evidence-based recommendations for improving health outcomes for lesbian, bisexual, and queer (LBQ) women as well as for trans and nonbinary (TNB) and intersex people. We draw on Egale’s recent research into the experiences of 2SLGBTQI people in accessing various healthcare and mental healthcare services. HESA should be aware of the unique healthcare needs and challenges of LBQ women and TNB and intersex people to ensure that these populations are adequately considered and represented in its work. The shortage of primary care physicians across Canada is significantly impacting 2SLGBTQI people. For example, 19% of 2SLGBTQI+ people in Canada do not have a primary care health provider (Women and Gender Equality Canada, 2023). This shortage is also having a negative effect on LBQ women and TNB people, who face myriad barriers to affirming and comprehensive primary, sexual, reproductive, and gender-affirming care (Jakubiec et al., 2023). Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health National Screening Guidelines and Canadian Women’s Healthcare
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health National Screening Guidelines and Canadian Women’s Healthcare A brief submitted on behalf of Responsible Healthcare Guidelines by: Shushiela Appavoo MD FRCPC, Associate Clinical Professor, University of Alberta Department of Radiology and Diagnostic Imaging [CONTACT] What are medical guidelines? Clinical guidelines are recommendations on how to diagnose and treat specific medical conditions. Guidelines are meant to ensure that patients receive appropriate diagnosis, treatment, and care. The Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care is an arm's length body, funded by Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC). It develops medical screening guidelines that are used to inform clinical practice across the country. Why are trustworthy national screening guidelines important to women’s health? Screening for early signs of common, significant and treatable diseases is known to decrease the incidence of advanced illness and death for many conditions. Several of the guidelines produced by the Canadian Task Force are particularly pertinent to women’s health. These guidelines include cancer screening (breast, lung, cervix) and non-cancer conditions (impaired vision, reproductive mental health). Inadequate screening results in avoidable late-stage disease, which has a negative impact on both quality of life and length of life. Many people assume that the national guideline body is led by experts who are pertinent to the conditions of interest. For example, people frequently assume that breast cancer experts are fully involved and even lead the guideline panels for breast cancer screening. Unfortunately, this is not true. The Task

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Screening for perinatal depression in
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Screening for perinatal depression in Canada A brief submitted on behalf of the [ORG] by: Catriona Hippman, PhD CGC, Postdoctoral Fellow, University of Calgary, BC Reproductive Mental Health Program Nicole Letourneau, PhD RN FCAHS FAAN FCAN FRSC, University of Calgary Research Excellence Chair in Parent and Child Health, Professor, Faculty of Nursing Lisa Gagnon, MD FRCPC, Psychiatrist, Perinatal Mental Health Network Chair, Alberta Health Services Prescilla Carrion, MSc, CGC, CCGC, Research Manager & Clinical Associate Professor, [ORG] Deirdre Ryan, MB FRCPC, Psychiatrist, Medical Director, BC Reproductive Mental Health Program Oct. 3, 2023 http://www.bcwomens.ca/our-services/specialized-services/reproductive-mentalhealth#Our--Services Phone: [CONTACT] Contacts: Dr. Deirdre Ryan, Dr. Catriona Hippman, Medical Director; Postdoctoral Fellow [CONTACT] [CONTACT] Background Perinatal depression is common; affecting 10-15% of women1–4. Rates are even higher amongst marginalized groups, affecting almost half (50%) of immigrant women5 and Indigenous women6. In light of this, it is unsurprising that suicide is a leading cause of maternal deaths in Canada7–9. Further, the costs of untreated perinatal depression and anxiety in the UK have been estimated at 6.6 billion GBP for births in the year 2013 alone10. In a 2021 report by the Canadian Perinatal Mental Health Collaborative, extrapolating these data to the Canadian population demonstrated a cost of 150,000 CAD for each mother-baby dyad affected by perinatal depression and anxiety11. However, perinatal depression can be successfully treated, particularly with early identification, and negative downstream consequences for the child and family can thus be prevented12

Name: The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Women’s Health A Submission to the Federal Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study (October 5, 2023)
card_text: The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Women’s Health A Submission to the Federal Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study (October 5, 2023) The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Supporting Women’s Health & Well-Being: Ensuring that women receive access to physiotherapy care in Canada is essential as it provides numerous health benefits including improved health outcomes, reduced healthcare costs, increased equality, empowerment, and self-autonomy. Physiotherapy professionals are highly skilled at addressing specific women’s health issues such as pelvic floor dysfunction, pregnancy-related musculoskeletal conditions, postpartum recovery, menstrual pain management, pre-and post-surgical rehabilitation, management of chronic pain conditions, osteoporosis management, breast health, and lymphedema. Recommendation 1: Ensure that women in Canada have equitable, publicly funded access to pelvic health physiotherapy. Physiotherapists play a crucial role in supporting women with a variety of needs, but access to pelvic health physiotherapy is limited despite being established as a first-line treatment for a variety of issues1. Pelvic health physiotherapy is well-established in many countries globally but continues to be an area of growth for the Canadian healthcare system. France, for example, has prioritized this care with a publicly funded pelvic floor rehabilitation program that provides post-natal patients with 10, and up to 20 pelvic health physiotherapy sessions. This unique program allows women to re-educate their pelvic floor following pregnancy and to resolve common issues such as urinary or fecal incontinence, pelvic organ prolapse, and pelvic pain conditions2 3. Name: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Linda Silas, President
card_text: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Linda Silas, President [ORG] Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the largest nurses’ organization in Canada, representing 250,000 frontline unionized nurses and nursing students across the country. Our members work in every sector of health care – from home care and long-term care to community and acute care – and practice as licensed/registered practical nurses, registered nurses, registered psychiatric nurses and nurse practitioners. Nurses are the largest regulated profession in health care with a total of 466,014 regulated nurses eligible to practice in Canada in 2022, as identified by the Canadian Institute for Health Information, and an estimated 90% of them are women.1 There are many workplace issues in health care that have significant health consequences for nurses, regardless of the individual nurse’s sex or gender. However, since a vastly disproportionate number of nurses are women, it is important to recognize these health issues as directly impacting women’s health in Canada. Amid a health human resources crisis, with record nurse vacancies in health workplaces across the country, it is even more imperative that the issues contained in this brief are addressed with robust support from the federal government. Mental health Before the pandemic, one in three nurses screened positive for major depressive disorder, one in four screened positive for generalized anxiety disorder and clinical burnout, one in three reported having suicidal thoughts, and over 20% screened positive for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and panic disorder. Name: June 15, 2023 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) House of Commons Ottawa, ON
card_text: June 15, 2023 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] via email: [CONTACT] Re: Women’s Health Study Dear Chair Casey and Distinguished Members of the Standing Committee on Health: On behalf of [ORG] and Environmental Defence, we are raising the gendered impacts of exposures to harmful product ingredients in personal care products as a disproportionate impact that should be addressed as part of your study of impacts to women’s health, and request that you provide officials with recommendations that support the Health Minister’s mandate to introduce mandatory labelling of harmful ingredients in consumer products. Ingredient transparency and disclosure not only provides individuals with the right-to-know what is in the products they use, it also drives product reformulation to safer chemicals. This is a large sector in Canada, with revenue in the Canadian personal care products market expected to generate $11 billion CAD in 2023 and is projected to grow significantly. Harmful substances must be phased out of these products, and ingredient transparency will help to drive transformation in this sector. As a mission-driven clean beauty brand, [ORG] has built a movement for improved transparency and accountability across the personal care products industry. Since our launch in 2013, [ORG] has led advocacy efforts across North America to update regulations that have not kept pace with a growing body of science that demonstrates that harmful chemicals do not belong in personal care products. Name: Brief to the 44th Canadian Parliament, 1st Session Committee on Women's Health Authored by Manvir Sandhu
card_text: Brief to the 44th Canadian Parliament, 1st Session Committee on Women's Health Authored by Manvir Sandhu Dear Members of the Committee, As we gather to discuss and enact legislation pertaining to the health and well-being of Canada's women, our commitment to inclusivity, equal opportunity, and the inherent dignity of every individual. This brief aims to provide concise yet comprehensive insights into the prevailing women's health issues in Canada and recommends specific amendments for our consideration. 1. Breast Cancer Breast cancer remains the most commonly diagnosed cancer among Canadian women, accounting for 25% of all new cases. There is an apparent necessity to ramp up efforts toward early detection and timely treatment. Recommendation: Increase funding for the Canadian Breast Cancer Screening Initiative and mandate coverage for regular mammograms for all women over the age of 40 by private and public health insurers. 2. Maternal Mortality While Canada enjoys one of the lowest maternal mortality rates globally, some communities, particularly Indigenous women, experience disproportionately higher rates. This disparity signals the need for a targeted approach. Recommendation: Allocate additional funding to the Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program, particularly in underserved regions. Strengthen cultural competence training for healthcare providers to address health disparities better. 3. Mental Health Canadian women report higher rates of mood and anxiety disorders than their male counterparts. Unfortunately, mental health issues remain underfunded and often misunderstood. Recommendation: Create a national strategy to increase funding and resources for women-specific mental health services, including trauma-informed care and support for victims of domestic violence.

[ORG] Canada works closely with health professionals, patient groups and decision makers to ensure that its health care solutions are accessible to the greatest number of patients. Equity and parity issues are also at the heart of the company’s concerns. The company has implemented sustainable business practices to reduce its impact on the environment and is actively involved in its community, for example with Le Chaînon in Montreal. Name: Women’s Health in Renfrew – Pembroke – Nipissing: The Case for Two Electoral Districts Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health in Canada Submitted by: L
card_text: Women’s Health in Renfrew – Pembroke – Nipissing: The Case for Two Electoral Districts Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health in Canada Submitted by: Lilly Kruzsely for [ORG] April 4th, 2023 Backgrounder: On March 29th, 2023, Renfrew County took the very important step to declare Intimate Partner Violent (IPV) an epidemic.1 The motion build upon Recommendation #1 from the rural-focused Coroner’s inquest into the murders of Carol Culleton, Anastasia Kuzyk, and Natalie Warmerdam.2 It is especially difficult to study the issue of Women’s health in Canada when new data from CTV suggests that a woman is murdered in Canada every 48 hours.3 In Renfrew County, 2 women have already been killed by men since December 2022. Dead women cannot speak to women’s health. Violence in the home is not a private matter. It affects the wellbeing of our communities and as members of the Standing Committee on Health, you have a responsibility to those experiencing violence to do everything that you can to stop it. This submission to the committee makes the case for the 1 Ontario MP federal electoral riding seat allocation increase for 2023 be used to create 2 electoral ridings out of the current constituency known as: Renfrew – Pembroke – Nipissing as a critical actionable item to addressing IPV in Renfrew County. Moreover, I strongly urge the committee to consider the impact that disproportionate representation of certain electoral ridings in Canada over others as a broader study factor

card_text: WHO WE ARE We are physicians, researchers, maternal-child health advocates, and members of the Manitoba Interdisciplinary Lactation Center (MILC). Our mission is to improve maternal, child and population health by fostering interdisciplinary collaborations and partnerships that support innovation, discovery, knowledge mobilization and policy development focused on human milk and infant feeding. MILC is co-Directed by Dr. Meghan Azad (PhD), Dr. Nathan Nickel (PhD, MPH) and Natalie Rodriguez (MBA, CCIP) at the University of Manitoba in Winnipeg, Canada. MILC members include Dr. Katherine Kearns and Dr. Christina Raimondi, co-founders of the North American Board of Breastfeeding and Lactation Medicine and the Winnipeg Breastfeeding Center - Manitoba’s first clinic dedicated to providing evidence-based diagnostic support to families who need help with lactation. We Propose Four Calls to Action 1. Increase funding for collection and accurate reporting of infant feeding data 2. Secure and standardize insured, equitable and evidence–based breastfeeding care and devices (such as breast pumps) that protect breastfeeding choice 3. Mandate clinical competency and accessible delivery of breastfeeding support and care in every Canadian community 4. We use the terms ‘breastfeeding’ and ‘lactation’ to represent women and gender nonconforming, trans, or nonbinary individuals (i.e. women+) who feed their infants human milk. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study September 9, 2022 Submitted by:
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study September 9, 2022 Submitted by: Benjamin Davis Senior Vice-President, Mission The Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Society of Canada Introduction The Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Society of Canada is pleased to provide this submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study on women’s health. Canada has one of the highest rates of multiple sclerosis (MS) in the world. An estimated 90,000 Canadians live with the autoimmune disease and, on average, 12 Canadians are diagnosed every day. Women are three times more likely to be diagnosed with MS than men. Most people are diagnosed with MS between the ages of 20 and 49 – the age at which many women are starting their careers and perhaps building a family – and the unpredictable effects of the disease will last for the rest of their lives. MS is classified as an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system. The immune system attacks myelin, the protective covering of the nerves in the brain, which disrupts communication between the central nervous system and the rest of the body. The disease course of MS is unpredictable, often occurring in a pattern of relapses and remissions, also known as an episodic disability. There is currently no cure for MS, though multiple disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) exist to help manage symptoms. The immense socioeconomic impact of MS for women The cost of paying for medication, services, equipment, and treatment is a significant economic burden for people with MS and their families.

Name: Submission To the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Assisted Human Reproduction Impacts Women’s Health Arthur Leader, MD, FRCSC *

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Submitted by Sabrina Flack & Dehga Scott
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Submitted by Sabrina Flack & Dehga Scott July 21, 2022 www.northernbirthwork.com [CONTACT] [CONTACT] [CONTACT]. Yellowknife, NT [CONTACT] (PO Box 2392) Purpose This submission details the unique role that birthworkers play in promoting positive health outcomes for pregnant and birthing people, children, families, and communities. It also provides an overview of the current landscape of abortion access in Canada’s North and outlines the role of birthworkers— specifically, abortion doulas—in providing abortion support. While this study is titled “Women’s Health,” we would like to acknowledge that many aspects of health and the health system that impact cis-gender women also affect people of all genders, including transgender men and non-binary people. Therefore, while not to detract from the gender-specific impacts of various policy decisions, the [ORG] chooses to employ terms that are inclusive of all persons who may experience pregnancy, birth, or birth-related concerns (i.e., “pregnant person”). Introduction What is Birthwork? Birthworkers, more commonly known as midwives or Doulas, work with individuals to provide full-circle support throughout the reproductive journey. Birth workers are unlike nurses, obstetricians, or midwives as they do not offer medical or obstetrical care. Birthworkers practice holistically by providing physical, emotional, mental, spiritual, and informational support for the pregnant person and their family. There have always been people in the community who assisted with reproductive care. Name: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Topic: How Agricultural Chemicals and Clinical Coding Gaps are Fueling Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Executive Summary
card_text: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Topic: How Agricultural Chemicals and Clinical Coding Gaps are Fueling Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Executive Summary Canada's strategy to fight Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) has two major blind spots. First, agricultural chemicals like glyphosate and neonicotinoids act as lowdose antimicrobials in the human gut. They force bacteria to adapt and become resistant to life-saving medical treatments. Health Canada's safety models miss this entirely because they evaluate systemic organ damage rather than localized gut bacteria disruption. Second, national data on AMR is fundamentally flawed. Tracking AMR carrier status (like MRSA) in hospitals is weighed down by administrative burdens and outdated records. We cannot fight an AMR crisis if our chemical safety laws ignore environmental root causes and our clinical data is incomplete. Part 1: The Chemical Blind Spot in the Gut Health Canada and the Pest Management Regulatory Agency (PMRA) calculate safety limits, such as the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI), based on systemic toxicity—meaning they look for measurable damage to organs. This model ignores the human microbiome. Glyphosate is poorly absorbed by the human body; the vast majority remains in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. The Target: Glyphosate targets the "shikimate pathway". Human cells do not have this pathway, but our essential gut bacteria do. The "Dilution Fallacy": PMRA calculates safety limits based on the weight of an entire human body. However, because the chemical pools in the gut, the concentration against the 200–500g microbiome is exponentially higher, meaning the calculation is flawed.

Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Antimicrobial Resistance [ORG] & [ORG]
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Antimicrobial Resistance [ORG] & [ORG] February 2026 Overview Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the most urgent threats to global and Canadian health, undermining the effectiveness of life-saving medicines and straining health systems. Drug-resistant tuberculosis (DR-TB) is a major driver of this crisis, causing substantial mortality worldwide. Although Canada’s TB burden is relatively low, cases have surged sharply, with DR-TB rising by 233% over the last 14 years, exposing vulnerabilities in surveillance, diagnostics, and treatment systems and amplifying domestic risk from global transmission. TB and AMR are deeply interconnected: both require robust infrastructure for detection, stewardship, and treatment. Tackling TB is therefore essential not only to control this deadly disease but also to strengthen Canada’s broader response to AMR and reinforce overall health system resilience. Key recommendations: 1. Integrate TB into the National AMR Framework Integrating TB will help leverage existing systems, prevent duplication, and promote faster, more effective antibiotic use. 2. Invest in Research and Development for TB and AMR Funding vaccines, diagnostics, and better treatments will reduce resistance and strengthen health systems and AMR preparedness globally and domestically. 3. Strengthen Global Health Partnerships and Investments Investing and aligning TB and AMR efforts will reduce international transmission of resistant strains while reinforcing domestic resilience. 4. Build resilient diagnostic, surveillance, and stewardship systems Using TB systems as a tracer for AMR to expand diagnostics, surveillance, stewardship, and equitable access, will create a model for a resilient and effective AMR response. 5. Promote health

Castonguay4 For: The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Presented by: François M. Castonguay Executive Summary Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a major threat to Canada’s health and economy. It is already the cause of thousands of deaths each year, and without stronger action, its health burden could more than double by 2050. The economic costs could rise from a few billion dollars today to tens of billions in 2050, which would be increasingly borne by sectors outside the health care system. The Pan-Canadian Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance provides a solid foundation, but to be fully effective, it must be part of an integrated framework such as the “One Health” approach, which links human, animal and environmental health. Combined with economics tools, this framework becomes a powerful lever for targeting efficient interventions, strengthening intersectoral cooperation, and directing investments based on their expected health and economic benefits. The Driver of Antimicrobial Resistance Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a cross-sectoral issue affecting human and animal health. It results from the widespread use of antimicrobials in human medicine and agriculture (livestock production, dairy farming and aquaculture), with possible dissemination via wastewater, soil, the food chain, wildlife or vectors [1]. Any use can promote the emergence of resistant pathogens, a well-documented phenomenon. While AMR is not a new issue, it is currently occurring

card_text: School of Public Health, Université de Montréal Department of Health Management, Evaluation and Policy The Socio-economic Impacts of Antimicrobial Resistance in Quebec by 2050 Prepared by: François Arès,1 Caroline Raymond2 and François M. Castonguay3 For: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, at the request of Mr. Maxime BlanchetteJoncas4 Executive Summary We have adapted the results of a Canadian study on the socio-economic impacts of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) to the Quebec context, while incorporating the demographic and economic differences between provinces. Without enhanced action, AMR could lead to 1,500 to 3,000 deaths per year by 2050, resulting in annual losses of $2.6 to $4.2 billion to Quebec’s gross domestic product (GDP), including $1.2 to $1.7 billion in additional costs for the health care system. Quebec would be more negatively affected than the Canadian average, underscoring the pressing need for concerted action to reduce the inappropriate use of antimicrobials. It is intended to inform public decisions with estimates adapted to the Canadian context. Using a One Health [2] approach—linking human, animal and environmental health—the report quantifies the health and economic impacts of AMR: mortality, costs to the health care system, and economic losses measured by a decline in gross domestic product (GDP). card_text: #200, [CONTACT] SE Calgary, AB [CONTACT] Tel [CONTACT] Fax [CONTACT] Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] February 9, 2026 Re: Input for study on antimicrobial resistance Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health, On behalf of the National Cattle Feeders’ Association (NCFA), thank you for the opportunity to provide input into your study on antimicrobial resistance. NCFA represents cattle feeders across Canada and advocates for policies that support animal health and welfare, food safety, and the long-term sustainability of the Canadian beef sector. As the Committee examines the drivers of antimicrobial use and resistance in Canada, it is important that the role of cattle feedlots is accurately understood and appropriately reflected in your analysis and recommendations. Canadian feedlots operate within a robust regulatory and stewardship framework and are subject to oversight by Health Canada and the Canadian Food Inspection Agency. Medically important antimicrobials are available by prescription only and are used under veterinary oversight through established veterinary-client-patient relationships, supported by record-keeping requirements and mandatory withdrawal periods to protect public health. Feedlots provide a highly controlled environment that enables close monitoring of cattle health, early disease detection, and targeted treatment when animals become ill. This structure supports responsible antimicrobial use by ensuring treatments are timely, precise, and based on veterinary guidance. Prompt treatment of sick animals is essential to maintaining animal welfare and reducing the spread of disease within a population, and should not be characterized as routine or indiscriminate antimicrobial use. Name: [ORG] The Essential Role of Veterinarians in Antimicrobial Stewardship in Canada For the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2025
card_text: [ORG] The Essential Role of Veterinarians in Antimicrobial Stewardship in Canada For the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2025 The [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits this brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health for its study on antimicrobial resistance (AMR). The aim is to inform the Committee about the role of veterinarians in antimicrobial stewardship (AMS), the system-level challenges they face, and opportunities to strengthen Canada’s public health response. Public Health Context: AMR and One Health AMR threatens human and animal health, food systems, and economic stability. In Canada, approximately 26% of human infections are resistant to commonly used treatments, with projections indicating rising resistance and increasing mortality and costs if additional action is not taken. AMR arises from complex interactions among humans, animals, and the environment. While antimicrobial use in both human and veterinary medicine contributes, transmission pathways vary by pathogen and setting. Evidence shows that a coordinated, cross-sectoral One Health1 approach is effective to reduce AMR while protecting animal welfare, public health, and the environment. Canada has established foundations through the Pan-Canadian Action Plan on AMR (PCAP) and integrated surveillance programs. However, assessments by the Auditor General of Canada and the Canadian Academy of Health Sciences identify gaps in coordination, coverage, and sustained investment. Veterinarians are essential partners in ensuring antimicrobial stewardship and protecting public health. Veterinary Role in Antimicrobial Stewardship The [ORG] represents veterinarians and allied professionals nationally. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Written Testimony by Leanne McConnachie [ORG] Canada Thank you, Madame Chair, and House Committee members, for the opportunity to submit the following brief. The information in this brief was also shared with the Standing Committee on Science and Research in October 2025 during their AMR study. I will address the overuse of antimicrobials in intensive animal agriculture. I am representing [ORG] Canada, a global animal welfare organization that, for decades, has advocated for better farmed animal welfare standards. [ORG] is also a member of Animal Health Canada’s National Farm Animal Care Council (NFACC). I personally have been involved in farm animal welfare issues for the past 20 years. Seventy percent (70%) of the world’s antibiotics are used in animal agriculture1, often administered prophylactically, and daily in-feed, to prevent disease.2 Prophylactic use refers to the administration of antimicrobials even when illness and harmful bacteria are not present. This is unlike metaphylactic use where bacteria or infection is nearby and disease spread is likely, or therapeutic use when infection is present and treatment of the animal is necessary. We fully support the use of antimicrobials for metaphylactic or therapeutic treatment, but not for prophylactic purposes. This principle has been put into effect in a number of jurisdictions which I will discuss shortly. Name: Evidence Brief: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in Canada, with a focus on infection control and surveillance needs in human healthcare systems. Prepared by Dr. Kevin J. Stinson, PhD CIC, on behalf of
card_text: Evidence Brief: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in Canada, with a focus on infection control and surveillance needs in human healthcare systems. Prepared by Dr. Kevin J. Stinson, PhD CIC, on behalf of Infection Prevention and Control Canada Submission to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Antimicrobial Resistance Meeting Date 02 December 2025 Submitted 16 December 2025 Executive Summary Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a global health concern already associated with significant mortality and financial impacts in Canada and abroad. AMR has been described as a “silent pandemic”, as cases and transmission often go unnoticed. Antimicrobial resistant organisms (AROs) have an ability to transfer resistance genes to bacteria of the human microbiota, thereby driving persistent colonization. Today, nearly one in 10 acute care patients in Canada is colonized with an ARO. Infection control, surveillance, and antimicrobial stewardship are three of the core pillars required to effectively combat AMR. Yet there are inconsistencies with the implementation of these programs across Canada, especially in underserved communities or healthcare sectors. Current surveillance programs in Canada act as sentinels to identify broad AMR trends but lack the depth or breadth needed to effectively map transmission, and current practices lack the granularity needed to inform local practices. Opportunities to address these challenges include building infection control capacities within the healthcare workforce, through training and capacity-building initiatives such as the IPAC Hub program currently operating across Ontario. As a long-term strategy, Canada must improve our AMR surveillance programs. We need an integrated, multi-sector approach, with a

card_text: Brief and Speaking notes for Dec 11, 2025 HESA meeting. Prepared by Kim Neudorf patient and family partner with [ORG] ([ORG]). Madam Chair and committee members thank you for this opportunity to share our recommendations. I represent [ORG]. We are a non-for-profit volunteer-driven organization representing patients and families, who have experienced harm within our publicly funded healthcare system. Our mission is Every Patient Safe. We view AMR, sepsis and healthcare associated infections, as a key patient safety issues. We collaborate on AMR research, help develop standards and resources, raise public and professional awareness, present to health professionals, and participate in policy discussions. Appendix A, outlines the breadth of our work over the past 12 months. With the family’s permission, I would like to share an experience that illustrates the profound human tragedy behind the science, and the clinical, economic and social burden of AMR. A healthy 70-year-old sustained a simple foot fracture. Within two days she developed severe pain beneath the cast, accompanied by concerning changes in her vital signs and cognition. When the cast was removed, her foot was gravely infected. Sepsis was eventually diagnosed. MRSA, the most common HCA AMR pathogen, was identified. What followed was 419 consecutive days of hospital care. She lost her foot, and averted amputation of her arm by two hours. Sepsis returned, as it does, and ultimately MRSA and sepsis claimed her life. The estimated healthcare costs incurred exceeded $750,000. Name: On the Front Line of Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
org: Canadian Pharmaceutical Manufacturers and Exporters Alliance
card_text: On the Front Line of Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Canadian Pharmaceutical Manufacturers and Exporters Alliance ([ORG]) Executive Summary Over the past two decades, domestic drug manufacturing has steadily declined in Canada. Today, Canada imports more than 80% of its medicines, leaving our healthcare system increasingly vulnerable to global supply disruptions, geopolitical instability, and foreign policy decisions beyond our control. This level of dependence is not only a public health concern - it is a national security risk that demands policy action and leadership. At the same time, the United States is moving rapidly to reshore pharmaceutical production and secure its domestic supply chain. In April 2026, the U.S. announced Section 232 tariffs on pharmaceutical imports, including potential 100% tariffs on patented medicines from countries without bilateral agreements. Canada is directly exposed, putting more than $9 billion in annual pharmaceutical exports at risk. The 232 tariffs are being reinforced by a coordinated set of domestic incentives designed to attract pharmaceutical manufacturing, including: • Regulatory prioritization for U.S.-based manufacturers • Reduced user fees, lower inspection costs, and market access priority for domestic producers • Targeted tax and investment incentives • Procurement policies favouring domestically produced medicines Canada now faces a critical inflection point. Without a clear and immediate policy response, investment will continue to shift to jurisdictions that offer stronger incentives and more predictable market conditions. Under current conditions, Canadian pharmaceutical manufacturers will face increasing pressure to move production out of Canada. Canada must act with

The brief emphasizes that Canada cannot relocate all pharmaceutical production, but it can strengthen certain strategic capacities where they would have real added value. Name: [ORG] Submission to HESA Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026
card_text: [ORG] Submission to HESA Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [CONTACT] | www.canadiangenerics.ca Introduction Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty begins with the generic and biosimilar medicines industries, which supply more than 80% of the prescription medications needs of Canadian patients. As a starting point, pharmaceutical sovereignty will require decision makers to recognize that cost-saving generic and biosimilar medicines are not just budget line items — they are strategic assets that must be treated as health, economic, and national security priorities. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is about protecting the health of Canadians through a reliable, secure, and sustainable domestic supply of medicines. It means ensuring that Canada has the domestic capacity and policy frameworks in place to reliably supply and produce essential medicines to meet the needs of Canadians. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed the fragility of global pharmaceutical supply chains. Border disruptions, export restrictions, shortages of active pharmaceutical ingredients, and geopolitical tensions revealed a simple truth: when supply chains fracture, Canadians are vulnerable. Pharmaceutical sovereignty will require government decision makers to prioritize actions to address the significant existing barriers to market entry for generic and biosimilar medicines. This is needed to both ensure a robust pipeline of new generic and biosimilar medicines for Canadians and to support domestic generic drug manufacturers who want to grow their manufacturing and R&D capacity here in Canada. The lack of predictability and significant risk companies are facing in Canada today is unprecedented. It is difficult for our member companies to plan and build a business case to make the

Improve Canada’s Drug Approval Pathway 3. Establish robust and sustainable clinical trial funding to make Canada the world’s fastest country to launch a clinical trial 4. Improve post-approval drug access pathways Executive Summary Pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved through domestic manufacturing alone. For life-saving medicines (particularly oncology therapies), Canada’s long-term security, affordability, and access depend on anchoring investment across the full medicines lifecycle, from discovery and clinical trials through to production and patient access. The [ORG] ([ORG]) urges the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) to recognize that a robust, internationally competitive clinical trials ecosystem is a foundational pillar of pharmaceutical sovereignty. Countries that succeed in hosting trials are more likely to attract downstream investment and ensure early and reliable access to innovative medicines for patients. In addition, pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved if medicines are approved but remain inaccessible to patients. This includes efficient health technology assessments (HTAs), coordinated price negotiation and timely and consistent public reimbursement across Canada. When access to essential medicines is delayed, fragmented, or dependent on ad hoc mechanisms, Canada remains vulnerable to external decision-making, market instability, and supply disruptions. A sovereign pharmaceutical system must ensure approvals lead reliably to timely, funded, and equitable patient access across the country. Name: Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Through Domestic Supply and Competitive Post-Patent Markets
card_text: Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Through Domestic Supply and Competitive Post-Patent Markets Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) [ORG] May 2026 Executive Summary Canada is not capturing the post-patent opportunity to supply its own medicines. While generic and equivalent medicines account for the majority of prescriptions dispensed in Canada, only a small proportion, estimated at 12% in 2022, are manufactured domestically. As a result, our country remains heavily reliant on multinational pharmaceutical companies for the supply of essential medicines. In a well-functioning system, the period following patent expiry should enable Canadian companies to compete, supply lower-cost alternatives and expand domestic production. In practice, this transition is not occurring. Market dynamics continue to favour large, non-Canadian firms, limiting competition and preventing domestic manufacturers from gaining meaningful market share. This dynamic also limits price competition, resulting in higher costs for public drug plans and Canadian patients, and limiting the savings that should be realized following patent expiry. This structural problem not only increases the risk of supply disruptions that can affect patient care, but also results in higher and unavoidable costs for the healthcare system at a time when governments can least afford unnecessary spending. This is not a question of capacity. Canadian companies exist, are growing and are already investing in domestic manufacturing. However, current market conditions do not provide the predictability or fairness required to support long-term investment in local production. The result is a structural weakness: Canada continues to rely on external suppliers even when

card_text: May 5, 2026 House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Executive Summary The [ORG] ([ORG]), now known as Vital - Canada’s Pharmaceutical Supply Chain Alliance, represents the companies responsible for delivering the vast majority of medicines across Canada. From this system-wide vantage point, pharmaceutical distributors have a unique understanding of how medicines move from global supply to patient care, and how policy decisions impact access. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is an important policy objective, often framed in terms of domestic manufacturing and reducing reliance on international suppliers. While these are important considerations, ensuring that medicines reach patients reliably requires a broader system perspective. Ultimately, pharmaceutical sovereignty is determined by whether medicines reach patients reliably, safely, and on time. Canada already has a critical national asset that supports this objective: a highly regulated, nationally integrated pharmaceutical distribution system that ensures physical access to medicines across the country every day. Distribution is the system that connects supply to patients. Distributors operate at the centre of the supply chain, connecting hundreds of manufacturers to over 12,000 points of dispensing. From this position, they see how policy decisions—on pricing, procurement, and manufacturing—directly affect access to medicines for Canadians. Canada will continue to rely on global supply chains for a significant share of its medicines. The policy objective should therefore be to ensure that this supply can be reliably accessed and delivered to patients. Vital recommends that the Committee: 1. Recognize pharmaceutical distribution as critical health system infrastructure and ensure that

Name: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
card_text: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty National pharmaceutical sovereignty ensures that Canadians have reliable access to highquality medicines and treatments when they need them. This means having a strong domestic manufacturing capacity and integration into key supply chains. Medicines and the systems that produce them sit at the intersection of public health, economic growth and national security. When a country’s pharmaceutical industry is dependent on foreign-owned firms and imported inputs, it may not reflect domestic priorities, leaving the country vulnerable to market forces and supply chain disruptions. Canada relies heavily on global supply chains for access to pharmaceuticals: almost all active pharmaceutical ingredients used in Canada are imported, with a majority from China and India, creating a concentration risk in critical inputs.i Canada also faces persistent and significant drug shortages. A stable supply is necessary to keep costs down, avoid expensive solutions to sudden shocks or emergencies and maintain access for all Canadians. The government should seek to bolster Canada’s biopharma capacity and self-suIiciency through targeted policy action and closer collaboration with industry. card_text: [ORG] Brief on “Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty” – Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Introduction [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits the following brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health regarding its study on “Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty.” This study comes at a pivotal moment for Canada’s life sciences sector, national security, and Canadian patients. The global pharmaceutical landscape is changing rapidly. The introduction of the most-favoured nation (MFN) drug pricing policy in the U.S., which aims to lower American prices and shift a greater share of innovation costs onto countries like Canada, has triggered a global chain reaction that is intensifying competition and influencing which countries get innovative medicines first. This move comes alongside President Trump’s recent executive order to impose a 100 per cent tariff on imported patented medicines and active ingredients. For Canada, this exposes a critical vulnerability: we have long benefited from U.S.- funded pharmaceutical innovation, while keeping our domestic drug prices low, ultimately limiting our availability of innovative medicines. Addressing this vulnerability starts with the fundamental premise that pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot happen in a vacuum. Any credible strategy must balance increased domestic capacity with the inherently global and interconnected nature of supply chains for innovative medicines. Canada has the foundation to get this right. Our innovative pharmaceutical industry is an economic engine that contributes $18.4 billion in economic impact, invests $3.2 billion in research and development, and supports over 110,000 high-value jobs. Underpinning this economic footprint are world-class researchers and academic institutions, and rigorous drug

Name: Submission Brief Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [ORG]
card_text: Submission Brief Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [ORG] Submitted to Standing Committee on Health (HESA) House of Commons Executive Summary The goal of strengthening Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty is widely shared, and strengthening domestic supply and improving patient access is critical to realize this goal. But successful pharmaceutical sovereignty requires a balanced, whole-system approach that includes all aspects of the innovation and pharmaceutical supply sectors. A narrow policy lens that favours domestic biomanufacturing in isolation must carefully consider downstream implications specifically on Canadians; those for whom these policies are being considered. Biomanufacturing is deeply connected with global supply chains, drawing on a complex, globally distributed network of innovators and suppliers, each providing critical components. Any policy that solely prioritizes domestic biomanufacturing, without an equal investment in other aspects of the pharmaceutical supply chain and innovation, can reduce competitive pressure, slow development, constrain supply and ultimately exacerbate the current risk of increased drug shortages as well as increasing costs and barriers to patient access. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is often discussed in terms of manufacturing capacity and upstream supply, but it must ultimately be measured by whether medicines reliably reach patients. True sovereignty is realized at the point of care. While strengthening domestic production and diversifying supply sources are important objectives, they are insufficient on their own if medicines cannot be accessed, substituted, or sustained when the system experiences disruption. Community pharmacies represent the last mile of Canada’s pharmaceutical system and are where fragility can impact health care. Every day, pharmacies manage shortages

Every day, our teams work within these frameworks – submitting evidence, navigating multiple review bodies, and coordinating with federal and provincial partners. This experience gives us a practical understanding of where the system supports timely access and where it places unnecessary strain on patients, regulators, and innovators. Supply chains have become more fragile, scientific advances are accelerating, and countries are moving quickly to modernize their regulatory systems to keep pace. International pricing initiatives, including the United States’ “Most Favored Nation” (MFN) drug pricing policy,1 have added another layer of uncertainty by increasing scrutiny on how countries make reimbursement decisions and how those decisions influence global launch and supply planning. Name: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: Andrew Booth, CFO
card_text: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: Andrew Booth, CFO May 5, 2026 Executive Summary Canada has the scientific foundation to be a global leader in biotechnology, yet it continues to capture only a fraction of the economic and strategic value generated by its own innovation. While Canadian researchers and companies are highly successful in discovering and advancing promising new therapies, these assets are too often sold to foreign firms prior to commercialization. This results in the loss of intellectual property (IP), high-value jobs, assets for national security, and long-term economic returns that could otherwise benefit Canadians. Pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved solely through supply chain measures or by relying on multinational firms to operate within Canada. Instead, it requires a deliberate strategy to build globally competitive, Canadian-headquartered biopharmaceutical companies that retain IP, scale domestically, and commercialize medicines from within Canada. Achieving this objective will require targeted policy reforms, including modernizing the SR&ED program to support growth-stage firms, implementing a Patent Box regime to incentivize IP retention, investing in critical infrastructure, and defining what constitutes a “Canadian Company” that is poised to scale up in a modern, globally integrated industry. card_text: May 5, 2026 Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Executive Summary The [ORG] ([ORG]) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the Standing Committee on Health’s (HESA) study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. Canada’s ability to ensure reliable, timely, and equitable access to medicines is a foundational element of both public health and economic resilience. From a patient perspective, pharmaceutical sovereignty must be understood not as isolation or domestic self-sufficiency, but as strategic resilience. This means the committee must consider how to protect access to medically necessary drugs across therapeutic areas, ensure continuity of supply, attract innovation and early launches, and deliver equitable access for all patients in Canada. This includes medicines for common chronic conditions, cancer and rare cancers, and therapies for rare diseases, which can be sensitive to market uncertainty, regulatory delays, and pricing instability. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is particularly consequential for Canadians living with cancer, rare cancers, and rare diseases, where treatment timelines are often narrow and therapies increasingly personalized. Delays in regulatory approval, clinical trial start-up, or reimbursement decisions can result in missed treatment windows, poorer health outcomes, and premature death. For patients with rare diseases, including rare cancers, where standard-of-care options may be limited or non-existent, access to innovative medicines and trials are often the only viable therapeutic pathway. For Canadians living with rare diseases, the concept of sovereignty is not abstract. It is directly tied to whether patients can rely on stable, predictable access to the therapies they need, many of which have no therapeutic alternatives and are produced through highly specialized global supply chains. Rare disease medicines represent some of the most advanced scientific innovations available today. They are also among the most vulnerable to disruptions in manufacturing, regulatory delays and unpredictable market conditions. When access breaks down, patients often have no fallback options. For this reason, strengthening pharmaceutical sovereignty for rare disease therapies is essential to ensuring that Canada can meet the needs of its most vulnerable populations. Canada has taken important steps in recent years, including the launch of the National Drugs for Rare Diseases (DRD) Strategy and the development of the Biomanufacturing and Life Sciences Strategy (BLSS). These initiatives have created a foundation for improved access and greater resilience. However, Canada continues to face challenges that affect its ability to secure timely and reliable access to rare disease medicines. These include slower approval and reimbursement timelines than peer countries, limited domestic manufacturing capacity for specialized therapies and increasing global competition for investment and product launches. card_text: Brief: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted to the Standing Committee on Health on April 29, 2026 Executive Summary [ORG] (CF Canada) is pleased to provide a brief to HESA regarding pharmaceutical sovereignty. Recent US pharmaceutical policy reforms and t threats of tariffs have created global instability and exposed structural vulnerabilities in Canada’s production and distribution of medicines, as well as in our drug review and reimbursement processes. For people living with cystic fibrosis (CF) and other rare diseases, these vulnerabilities may result in limited clinical trial access, delayed and/or reduced drug launches, and inequitable coverage of medicines across jurisdictions. Pharmaceutical sovereignty must be understood not as isolation or self-sufficiency, but as strategic resilience: the ability to secure timely access to innovative medicines, protect supply chains, and position Canada as a competitive jurisdiction for biomedical research, manufacturing, and early launch of and access to transformative therapies, including gene and cell therapies. For rare diseases like CF, where patient populations are small and drug development costs may be high, sovereignty must also mean alignment of global and domestic policies and processes, regulatory efficiency, and predictable access pathways. Drawing on evidence from HESA’s study on pharmaceutical sovereignty and CF Canada’s direct engagement with federal, provincial and territorial drug agencies, this brief outlines how Canada can strengthen pharmaceutical sovereignty in ways that prioritize patients with rare diseases like CF, while reinforcing Canada’s broader health and innovation objectives. card_text: Submission to the HESA Committee studying Canadian Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Canada: The Case for Domestic Manufacturing of Insulin and Essential Diabetes Medicines Submitted by [ORG] Pharmaceutical sovereignty refers to Canada’s ability to secure reliable and affordable access to essential medicines through domestic manufacturing, diversified supply chains, and strategic procurement. For the more than 4 million Canadians living with diabetes1, uninterrupted access to insulin, glucagon and other treatments are essential2. Name: Rebuilding Canada’s Insulin Production Capacity to Ensure National Health Security Submission to Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
card_text: Rebuilding Canada’s Insulin Production Capacity to Ensure National Health Security Submission to Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty By [ORG] Canada April 2026 Background & Context Discovered by Canadian scientists in 1921, insulin was produced domestically for 65 years at Toronto’s Connaught Laboratories, supplying Canadians affordably. However, after Connaught’s privatization in the 1980s, Canada ceased all insulin manufacturing by the mid-1990s and now imports 100% of its insulin1. Today, all insulin used by Canadians comes from a few large companies based outside Canada – notably Eli Lilly (U.S.), Novo Nordisk (Denmark), and Sanofi (France) – which produce insulin in their facilities abroad. This complete import reliance means Canada is fully dependent on foreign supply chains for a life-sustaining medication. Importantly, this paper does not recommend replacing or scaling down Canada’s existing insulin importation system. Canada’s current supply relies appropriately on trusted international manufacturers, and those relationships should continue. Rather, this submission recommends that Canada establish domestic manufacturing capability as a strategic contingency to be activated in emergencies, prolonged shortages, or major global disruptions so that Canadians are never left without access to insulin. Immediate Life-Saving Need Insulin is not just another pharmaceutical product; it is a critical, non-substitutable medicine. About 300,000 Canadians live with type 1 diabetes, meaning they cannot survive without daily insulin injections. For these individuals, any significant interruption in insulin access becomes life-threatening within days. Name: Built on U.S. Plasma: Canada’s Sovereignty Problem A Brief on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Plasma-Derived Therapies April 16, 2026
card_text: Built on U.S. Plasma: Canada’s Sovereignty Problem A Brief on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Plasma-Derived Therapies April 16, 2026 Dear Members of the HESA Committee: This brief concerns pharmaceutical sovereignty with respect to plasma-derived medicinal products (PDMPs), especially immunoglobulin. These medicines are essential for patients with immune deficiencies and other serious conditions, and they depend on a very large and reliable supply of collected plasma. Canada remains heavily dependent on plasma collected in the United States. If Canada is to be sovereign over this part of the pharmaceutical supply chain, there is no feasible path apart from welcoming a larger domestic commercial plasma collection sector. This dependence is more serious given rising demand for immunoglobulin, one of the medicines made from plasma. Our need for this medicine has nearly doubled over the past ten years, and is on track to nearly double again over the next ten years. I write as an ethicist and public policy scholar who has conducted extensive comparative research on plasma collection, donor safety, and plasma self-sufficiency across Canada, the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Europe, including the UK. Sincerely, Peter M. Date: April 2026 Executive summary Canada’s dependence on foreign pharmaceutical manufacturing, with 88% of generic medicines imported, exposes the country to rising global instability, fragile supply chains, and growing access gaps. Canada reports more active drug shortages than most OECD peers, while key supplier countries show high rates of regulatory non-compliance. The pandemic and ongoing geopolitical tensions have made clear that medicines are not ordinary goods but strategic assets essential to national security, economic stability, and public health. Pharmaceutical sovereignty means ensuring that Canadians have reliable, secure, and sustainable access to medicines through a strong domestic manufacturing and innovation base, supported by policies that keep Canadian production viable and competitive. True pharmaceutical sovereignty requires regulatory frameworks that accelerate access without disadvantaging Canadian producers, procurement tools that recognize domestic value-added, and targeted fiscal measures that make long-term investment in Canada attractive and sustainable. [ORG] recommends a coordinated policy package: 1. Recognize medicines as national security goods, enabling procurement and investment tools aligned with Canada’s sovereignty objectives. 2. Modernize regulation in a way that strengthens, not weakens, Canadian capacity, including a priority review pathway for Canadian developers and a narrow, high-value scope for the Reliance Order. 3. Reward domestic value-added through procurement criteria and fiscal measures, including a refundable profit-based- manufacturing tax credit, and federal investment incentives inspired by Québec’s Investment and Innovation Tax Credit (C3I), which supports modernization and advanced manufacturing upgrades (automation,

[CONTACT], Suite 204 Mississauga, ON [CONTACT] www.drreddys.com Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health for the Study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted on April 16, 2026 Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories Canada Inc. appreciates the opportunity to provide input to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health as part of its study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty. Introduction Pharmaceutical sovereignty is a shared objective: ensuring that Canadians have reliable, timely, and affordable access to essential medicines, including during periods of global disruption. From the perspective of a global generic pharmaceutical manufacturer with a strong Canadian footprint, Dr. Reddy’s believes this objective is best achieved through a balanced and pragmatic approach that strengthens domestic capacity while fully leveraging trusted global manufacturing and regulatory partnerships, supported by an efficient and predictable regulatory environment. Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories offers the following recommendations on securing Canada’s Drug Sovereignty. 1. Place access to generics at the centre of pharmaceutical sovereignty discussions. 2. Pursue a balanced strategy that integrates domestic and global manufacturing. 3. Work towards more predictable Health Canada approval timelines. 4. Promote supplier diversity rather than concentration. 5. Leverage trade and international regulatory cooperation. The Central Role of Generics in Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Generics play an essential role in Canada’s health system, delivering the majority of prescription volumes and generating significant cost savings for public and private payers. Canada's discussion of pharmaceutical sovereignty has focused predominantly on supply-chain security: securing access to medicines manufactured elsewhere. That framing, while important, addresses only half the question. True pharmaceutical sovereignty also requires the capacity to create novel therapies on Canadian soil — to move Canadian discoveries from the laboratory to the clinic and into patients' hands. On that dimension, Canada's performance is measurably poor, and the cause is structural. In December 2025, [ORG] commissioned the Institute on Governance (IOG) to benchmark Canada's translational health research ecosystem against six international comparators. The finding is unambiguous: Canada does not lack excellent science or policy effort. It lacks the governance infrastructure to convert discovery into therapy. Advanced therapies — including the cell and gene therapies that represent the next generation of medicine — fall structurally between federal mandates: too applied for federal health research funding, too risky for private capital. The consequences are visible: only 3.4% of cancer immunotherapy clinical trials conducted in Canada between 2002 and March 2026 were based on made-in-Canada innovations. card_text: A Brief to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) March 27, 2026 Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Resilience Building Capacity Along the Value Chain in a Context of Strategic Interdependence Introduction Recent disruptions in supply chains have reignited discussions about the resilience of health care systems. The COVID-19 pandemic, geopolitical tensions and the concentration of certain industrial capacities have exposed how vulnerable many countries are to disruptions in the supply of drugs and health products. The conclusion is clear: Canada must take action to strengthen its position in this strategic sector. This means enhancing its research and development capacity, better capturing and retaining the value of domestically developed innovation and supporting robust industrial capacities. At the same time, it is essential to improve the conditions for accessing drugs to ensure that the health care system operates smoothly. In an environment marked by increasing geopolitical tension, a systemic approach is needed to strengthen pharmaceutical resilience in the long term. Part of the solution lies in developing local production capacities in several components of the pharmaceutical value chain. Several countries have taken steps in this direction to better secure their supply of health care products.1 However, the pharmaceutical industry is extremely globalized. The various stages of research, development and production are spread across multiple countries, and this international organization of value chains is now a structural characteristic of the sector. card_text: [CONTACT] West Toronto, ON [CONTACT] Canada doctorswithoutborders.ca medecinssansfrontieres.ca March 16, 2026 Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health (HESA), RE: [ORG]/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) Submission – Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Introduction Tackling the topic of Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty necessitates recognizing that Canada is affected by events beyond its borders over which it does not have full control, whether the emergence and spread of a new disease that makes Canadians ill, or access to the medicines that can make them better. Similarly, decisions made in Canada, such as the recent cuts to global health under the federal international assistance budget, can have impacts on both the spread of disease and access to medicines abroad. As a medical humanitarian organization operating in over 70 countries, MSF witnesses firsthand the impact of outbreaks and epidemics as they transcend national borders, as well as the deadly consequences of inequities in access to medicines, vaccines and other health tools. The submission below emphasizes the fact addressing Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty must look outward as well as inward to effectively address Canada’s place within the larger world. In making this submission, MSF expresses concern that the Government of Canada has increasingly framed pharmaceutical development and production as an economic driver rather than as a means to benefit society across numerous metrics by keeping people healthy. card_text: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty and Insulin Supply in Canada Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) by Independent Voices for Safe & Effective Drugs March 13, 2026 Executive Summary Insulin is one of the most important medicines in modern healthcare. For the 640,000 Canadians living with diabetes who need it, uninterrupted access to insulin is essential for survival. For those with Type 1 diabetes, an auto-immune disease, there are no substitutes for insulin and without it they will die within days or weeks. Despite insulin’s importance—and its discovery in Canada—we currently have no domestic manufacturing capacity for this medicine. Instead, Canadians rely entirely on three foreign corporations that control 95% of the global supply of insulin. Recent developments in the global insulin market, including the discontinuation of multiple insulin products for commercial reasons, illustrate the dangers of relying exclusively on global pharmaceutical supply chains for essential medicines. The World Health Organisation estimates that half of the world’s population who need insulin either cannot afford it or have no access to supplies. Other jurisdictions have begun responding to these risks by strengthening domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing capacity and treating certain medicines, including insulin, as strategic assets. Canada is not currently among these. Until the mid-1980s, however, Canada did maintain domestic production of insulin and other biologics through publicly owned Connaught Laboratories. Re-establishing domestic insulin manufacturing capacity—preferably under public ownership and accountability—would strengthen Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty while ensuring secure access to one of the country’s most important medicines. card_text: Establishing a domestic supply chain for critical immunoglobulin therapy in Canada [ORG] operates the blood supply system in all provinces and territories except Quebec. Through an arm’s-length governance model, it is accountable to provincial and territorial ministers of health, who are its corporate members, and who provide operational funding. [ORG] is regulated by Health Canada. Within this framework, the organization is responsible and accountable to act in the best interests of the country by ensuring the safety and security of the supply of blood and blood products. As part of these responsibilities, the organization also manages a pan-Canadian formulary of about 50 plasma protein and related products, or PPRP, including immunoglobulin therapy made from human plasma. Human plasma carries immune-supporting proteins that specialized pharmaceutical companies manufacture into immunoglobulins through a process called fractionation. When someone's immune system fails, doctors turn to immunoglobulin therapy to provide antibodies to treat infections and immune-related conditions like primary immunodeficiencies, autoimmune diseases, and others. It is estimated that more than half (50 to 60 per cent) of patients in Canada prescribed immunoglobulins must take them to live, with no other treatment option available for their care. At a time when Canada’s governments and residents are focused on greater self-reliance — securing essential supply chains, building manufacturing capacity, and reaffirming the country’s sovereignty — [ORG] is implementing a comprehensive plan in keeping with these goals to ensure patients continue to have long-term access to immunoglobulins. card_text: Policy brief for --Reducing pressures on Canada’s healthcare industry Lok Bhattarai, PhD A stark mismatch in demand and supply of Canadian health-services is visible and is an ‘experiential reality’ rather than an ‘uncommon experience’. There is a complex relation between the augmenting healthcare demand, Canada’s immigration policy and integration of internationally trained professionals into the workforce. To address this problem, Canada can undertake several smaller fixes which altogether can produce greater impact in the long run. Below, I have discussed some of the cost-effective fixes which I believe can yield impressive results. Regulating doctor appointments and walk-ins: It is often reported that many family doctors or the doctors offering walk-ins often ask their patients to limit on only one health issue to consult per visit. In other words, doctors are reluctant to discuss more than one issue with the patients in one visit regardless of how minor or interconnected those issues are. This is one of the practices that instead of resolving healthcare burden is contributing to escalating artificial burden in the industry. Consequently, number of visits multiplies as patients may need to visit doctors more than once (maybe several times) where the issues could have been consulted in the first visit in a matter of an additional few more minutes.
