Introduction The [ORG] (NPAC-AIIPC) submits this brief to inform the Standing Committee on Health’s study on the impact of immigration policy on Canada’s health care system, with specific attention to nurse practitioners (NPs). Canada is experiencing sustained health workforce shortages and growing unmet needs in primary care, mental health, chronic disease management, and rural and remote services. Nurse practitioners, advanced practice nurses who independently assess, diagnose, order and interpret tests, prescribe, and manage care across the life span, are a proven way to expand access and improve quality. Federal immigration policy increasingly recognizes health workers, including NPs, as a priority occupation. However, misalignment between immigration pathways, credential recognition, and the availability of funded NP positions contributes to underutilization of internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) and missed opportunities to improve access to care. 2. They support disease management in primary care for conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), provide critical care in Canadian hospitals, and deliver ongoing care in the community for patients requiring complex respiratory disease management including home oxygen and mechanical ventilation. There are approximately 12,000 respiratory therapists working in Canada.1 Even before the COVID pandemic, and increasingly in its aftermath, Canada’s Occupational Projection System (COPS) Occupational Outlook identified a “Strong Risk of Shortage” of respiratory therapists between 2024-2033 at a national level. This shortage is likely to intensify: As frontline care providers to critically ill patients, the COVID pandemic has taken its toll on respiratory therapists. Alarmingly, respiratory therapists are showing worryingly high rates or signs of PTSD, symptoms of depression, and anxiety or stress. During the pandemic, more than 25% of respiratory therapists in Canada who were surveyed reported that they were considering leaving healthcare.12 Respiratory therapists are essential for addressing backlogs and access challenges across Canadian health systems. A shortage in the respiratory therapist workforce threatens Canadians’ access to essential healthcare, particularly lifesaving emergency and critical care, and chronic disease management. card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Study on the Impact of Immigration on Health Care November 28, 2025 Section 1: Introduction A strong and sustainable healthcare workforce is essential to delivering high-quality cancer care in Canada. Healthcare providers – including those on the frontlines and in supportive roles – bring a range of skills and expertise that directly impact access, quality and continuity of care. With over 80 professions involved in cancer care, ensuring an adequate, well-trained and well-distributed workforce is a critical priority which can be aided by strategic immigration policy. Canada’s healthcare system is facing unprecedented pressures due to an aging population, rising cancer incidence and persistent gaps in workforce distribution. These challenges are especially acute in rural and remote communities, where access to timely and specialized care is often limited. As the demand for cancer services grows, it becomes increasingly important to ensure that our healthcare workforce is not only sufficient in number but also equipped with the diverse skills and professional competencies needed to serve everyone in Canada. Strategic immigration policy, alongside federal investments in training and workforce mobility, is essential to building a resilient system that can adapt to evolving healthcare needs. Executive Summary: Diagnostic medicine is a vital but often less recognized component of the Canadian healthcare system, which has been increasingly under strain for the last five years. One important aspect of the diagnostic ecosystem in Canada is sonography. Sonography is the practice of using ultrasound as a medical investigation to assess organs, tissues, and blood flow inside the body. Diagnostic medical sonographers perform at least 10 million ultrasound scans in Canada each year. These highly skilled professionals are a vital part of the care economy and consistently enable improved health outcomes for Canadians, from diagnosis through patient care. Sonographers have been bearing the brunt of a medical system under strain, resulting in a shortage of Canadian-trained sonographers who are ready to enter active practice. However, there is a solution to this issue in supporting and funding programs for internationally trained medical professionals to transition into Canadian sonography practice. Current Situation: Sonography is practiced differently around the world. In most countries, sonography is performed by physicians who have the essential skills to become sonographers in Canada, should they wish to do so. With fundamental medical skills in place, most only need to familiarize themselves with Canadian practice norms and standards and acclimate to the Canadian health care system to succeed. card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Immigration, IEHP Integration, and Solutions to Accelerate Workforce Entry Submitted by [ORG] ([ORG]) and [ORG] November 2025 Executive Summary Canada is facing one of the most significant health human resource (HHR) shortages in its history. At the same time, nearly 260,000 internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) currently reside in Canada—yet only 58% are working in health-related occupations. Although Canada’s immigration system successfully attracts highly skilled health professionals, our regulatory, licensure, and workforce systems do not integrate them efficiently or effectively. This represents a profound missed opportunity at a time when Canada urgently needs physicians, nurses, pharmacists, dentists, and other essential providers. Evidence from the [ORG] ([ORG]) and its partners, including the PEGASUS Institute, shows that IEHPs experience fragmented licensure pathways, outdated or inaccurate information, reduced newcomer supports due to federal program cuts, and limited access to mentoring or professional networks. These challenges delay their entry into the workforce, erode clinical skills, and create financial and emotional strain for newcomers and their families. While reforms to immigration, regulatory, and licensing systems are underway, IEHPs who are already in Canada—and those who have begun their immigration journey—require targeted support to navigate the system, progress toward licensure, and optimize their contributions to Canada’s health workforce. This submission outlines key barriers and presents three evidence-based recommendations: 1. card_text: Written Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Systemic Barriers Facing Internationally Trained Dentists (ITDs) in Canada and the Need for Federal Oversight of the National Dental Examining Board (NDEB) Submitted by Seyedeh Fereshteh Morsali Internationally Trained Dentist (ITD) November 2025 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Internationally Trained Dentists (ITDs) in Canada face severe and well-documented barriers in their attempts to enter the dental workforce, despite Canada’s urgent need for qualified health-care professionals. The National Dental Examining Board of Canada (NDEB), which oversees the equivalency and licensing process, has created a system that is widely perceived by candidates as inconsistent, subjective, and lacking the transparency expected of a national regulatory body. These issues are particularly evident in the NDECC (Skills Exam), where candidates report unpredictable scoring, unrealistic clinical conditions, and an exam structure that does not reflect real-world dentistry. The consequences are significant: highly trained, experienced dentists—many with over a decade of clinical practice—are repeatedly failing an exam that often appears to reward inexperience rather than competence. Candidates face financial strain, emotional distress, career instability, and prolonged under-employment, frequently being forced into low-skill or low-wage jobs despite possessing advanced professional qualifications. These systemic issues affect thousands of internationally trained professionals across Canada and directly undermine federal goals related to immigration, labour market integration, and health-care capacity. I. Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) represents all 19 medical schools in the country and was pleased to provide testimony at the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health hearings on November 20, 2025. The purpose of this written submission is to build upon the important topics and questions raised at that hearing, and to provide the Committee with: - Key definitions and data points - Insights into the lack of capacity in undergraduate medical education - Ways to integrate more IMGs and FTPs into Canada’s existing training system II. card_text: Submission to The Standing Committee on Health Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by the [ORG] November 24, 2025 Introduction Canada’s ability to deliver timely, high-quality healthcare to children and youth increasingly depends on the strength of its highly specialized pediatric workforce. Today, that workforce is atrisk. Across the country, pediatricians and pediatric subspecialists—whose expertise is essential when children require preventive care, specialized treatments, or coordinated diagnosis and management of complex conditions—are in short supply. In addition to our important national focus on primary care, we must also recognize increasing subspecialty shortages which are directly limiting access to medically necessary care and compromising long-term health outcomes for Canadian infants, children and teenagers. This challenge is not abstract. More than 8 million children and youth currently live in Canada, a number expected to grow by 24% over the next fifty years. Yet across many jurisdictions, children now wait longer than adults for essential care, not because their needs are less urgent, but because the workforce trained to meet those needs is too small, aging, and increasingly stretched to meet the needs of an increasingly complex population of kids. Why Pediatric Workforce Sustainability Matters Children are not tiny adults. Children have unique physiological, developmental, and social needs that require dedicated training and coordinated care. Pediatric subspecialties—such as neonatology, pediatric neurology, gastroenterology, cardiology, oncology, and developmental pediatrics—form highly interdependent systems and are not inter-changeable with subspecialists who care for adults. card_text: [ORG]: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare Nov 25, 2025 Executive Summary Recent changes to immigration policy are beginning to impact [ORG]’s ability to sustain care delivery. With over 8,000 staff providing approximately 25,000 healthcare visits daily, [ORG] has relied on more than 650 temporary foreign workers to address persistent workforce shortages and expand services in high-need areas. However, a growing number of work permit renewal denials now threaten this critical staffing stream. If the trend continues, it could significantly reduce [ORG]’s capacity to meet rising demand - particularly as Canada’s aging population accelerates. The potential loss of these workers would result in loss of around 1,800 health care interactions daily, impacting thousands of Canadians. [ORG] urges this committee to recommend that the House of Commons address the urgent need for policy changes to support the continued use of temporary foreign workers in the healthcare system. Impacts of Immigration Policy Changes Canada is experiencing a persistent health human resources shortage that is placing growing pressure on the home and community care sector. As the population ages and demand for services rises, the limited supply of trained healthcare professionals, particularly nurses and personal support workers, is making it increasingly difficult to sustain high-quality care in the community. Recruitment and retention challenges, compounded by regional disparities and workforce burnout, have led many providers to rely on temporary or internationally trained workers to fill critical gaps. card_text: November 19, 2025 Catherine Ngando Edimo Clerk of the committee – HESA [CONTACT] Re: HESA Committee Study on the Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals On behalf of the [ORG] ([ORG]), I would like to thank HESA for this study and the opportunity to provide input in writing. Founded in 1995, the [ORG] ([ORG]) is the alliance of Canada’s pharmacy regulators – the 13 provincial and territorial pharmacy regulatory authorities (PRAs) across the country as well as the Canadian Forces Health Services. [ORG]’s Members regulate the practice of pharmacy in their respective jurisdictions in Canada, and their primary mandate is to protect and serve the public interest. [ORG] facilitates collaboration and national approaches among its Members as they address common issues in the practice of pharmacy in Canada. The provincial and territorial PRAs govern the pharmacy professions so that they best support the public in realizing optimal health outcomes. As part of that mandate, PRAs license pharmacy professionals (pharmacists and pharmacy technicians), set required standards and codes of ethics for pharmacy practice, and investigate and resolve complaints in their respective jurisdictions. In addition to their core work of establishing and implementing licensure pathways, and ensuring adherence to professional standards, PRAs also explore opportunities that would allow them to better serve the public interest. Pharmacy professionals are the third largest group of health professionals in Canada, after nurses and physicians0F1, with over 50,600 registered/licensed pharmacists and over 11,500 registered/licensed pharmacy technicians across the country.

We have also seen skilled physicians trained abroad who are eager to help, but unable because of the barriers that stand in their way. The [ORG] views this study as a natural extension of the conversation our country must have about health workforce planning. Three years later, health workforce planning remains fragmented while demand for care continues to climb. More than 13,000 internationally trained physicians in Canada are not working in their field. card_text: ® Written Submission to The Standing Committee on Health Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by the [ORG] November 2025 Category Based Selection: Considerations for Dentistry Canada is currently facing a significant health human resource challenge, and the Government of Canada is taking decisive action to attract the expertise and talent required to meet the health needs of Canadians. One important initiative has been the prioritization of health-related occupations through the Express Entry system. While this approach appropriately seeks to strengthen Canada’s health workforce, the inclusion of dentistry in dedicated rounds of invitations for healthcare professionals may unintentionally create unrealistic expectations among prospective immigrants regarding their ability to obtain licensure and practise as dentists in Canada. Under an Act of Parliament, the [ORG] ([ORG]) has a statutory obligation to certify only those candidates who meet the established national standard of competence. All individuals— regardless of citizenship or country of education—must successfully complete the [ORG]’s Certification Process prior to licensure in Canada. Canada’s dental profession maintains reciprocal agreements with a limited number of countries, including the United States, Ireland, Australia, and New Zealand. These agreements recognize the equivalency of dental education and facilitate licensure for graduates of accredited programs in those jurisdictions. However, internationally trained dentists (ITDs) from countries without such reciprocal recognition must demonstrate equivalency through one of two routes: 1. As the population ages—particularly with the number of Canadians aged 85 and older expected to double by 2040—demand for LTC will rise dramatically. Yet, according to Statistics Canada (2024), there are already over 25,000 vacant LTC positions across the country1. Domestic recruitment and training initiatives remain essential but will take time to have an effect. Expand and simplify immigration pathways—for example, by creating a healthcarespecific pilot for LTC. This could build on strengths and lessons learned from the Home Care Worker Immigration Pilot and the Atlantic Immigration Program to attract and retain internationally educated health professionals specifically interested in working in LTC . 3. Create a healthcare-specific work permit that is Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) exempt. card_text: Written submission to the Standing Committee on Health Addressing Workforce Challenges in Dermatology Submitted by the [ORG] November 4, 2025 Introduction Canada's dermatologist community issued early warnings in the 2000s regarding a critical shortage of dermatologists1. The profession aimed to pre-empt the significant demographic shifts now evident in 2025, specifically a rapidly increasing aging population alongside a dermatology workforce shortage2. Despite these warnings, proactive solutions were not implemented, leaving us in a far more difficult position today. The health human resource (HHR) shortage is one that is prevalent throughout the entire Canadian health care system and extends globally. HHR recruitment and retention challenges have been building for years and were significantly intensified by the pressures of COVID-193. Across the system, gaps in workforce planning, training capacity, and provider well-being are straining the delivery of timely care. Dermatology is a critical component of an effective health care system and has been directly affected by these system pressures. The skin is the body’s largest organ and plays a vital role in overall health, providing a first line of defense against infection and environmental hazards. When skin is diseased, the impacts can be severe. Conditions like skin cancer require rapid diagnosis and treatment to prevent death, while non-lifethreatening conditions such as eczema, psoriasis, or severe acne can lead to chronic pain, physical discomfort, and serious mental health and systemic consequences4. Name: How the Government of Canada can increase the supply of family doctors
study_title: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals
card_text: How the Government of Canada can increase the supply of family doctors Overview Canada has a primary care crisis. Some 6.5 million Canadians do not have a family doctor. The federal government can rapidly and cost-effectively help mitigate this crisis by funding Practice Ready Assessment (PRA) programs across Canada. PRA programs provide an accelerated pathway to licensure for internationally-trained physicians (ITPs), thousands of whom are currently living in Canada as permanent residents and citizens. ITPs have completed their medical school and residency and have practiced medicine abroad. They have met all Canadian medical licensing requirements except for one: a competency assessment. This can be provided through a PRA which can be completed in 12 weeks or less. Successful completion of a PRA indicates that the ITP has demonstrated the ability to provide medical care at Canadian standards. Proposal We propose the federal government fund 4,500 PRA spots across Canada over a 4-year period. Given the average family physician sees 1,500 patients per year, this would give each currently waiting Canadian access to a family doctor by the end of the 4-year period. The proposal is efficient, since becoming licensed through a PRA is much quicker than the 2-year period to complete a medical residency. The proposal is also more costeffective than funding residencies–which cost about $340,000 per spot. The average cost for each 12-week PRA is only $35,000. card_text: [ORG] Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals October 2025 Underutilized Health Care Skills in Canada Health care systems across Canada are in crisis, facing critical shortages of qualified health professionals to respond to increased demands for care, particularly in small and mid-sized communities. The federal government anticipates a shortage of 117,600 nurses by 2030 and 20,000 family doctors by 2031. Meanwhile, thousands of internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) living in Canada are ready and qualified to help. Many are Canadian citizens and permanent residents. However, systemic barriers to licensure, certification, and workforce integration continue to limit their ability to contribute to their full potential. Statistics Canada data suggest that roughly 47% of internationally educated health professionals are either unemployed or working outside their field. The reasons for this are well understood and well-documented. They include insufficient access to training positions (for example, medical residency training), barriers to maintaining recent practice experience, high fees associated with assessments and licensing processes, insufficient access to bridging or laddering pathways, lengthy timelines, and the complexity of information about licensing pathways. Canada invites health care professionals to the country through our immigration system and invests in programs to support their integration through federal and provincial immigration dollars, as well as health care, employment and education funds. card_text: Written Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Addressing Workforce Challenges in the Oral Health Workforce Submitted by the [ORG] October 2025 Addressing Workforce Challenges in the Oral Health Sector Across Canada, the oral healthcare sector is grappling with serious human resources challenges, similar to those seen across the broader healthcare system. Dental clinics are struggling to hire and retain dental assistants and dental hygienists. Even prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, dentists were reporting significant concerns regarding their ability to recruit and retain well-qualified staff, particularly dental assistants. By 2019, roughly one-third of dental offices had vacant dental assistant positions, and this issue was exacerbated by the pandemic. Further, as Canada’s population continues to age, there will be an increase in demand and added pressures for dental offices. According to a recent Statistics Canada survey, 80% of dental offices in Canada are reporting staffing challenges. 46.5% of dental offices are having difficulties in recruiting dental assistants and 50.% are having difficulties in recruiting dental hygienists. In 2023 alone, over 7,200 job vacancies (dental assistants and dental hygienists) went unfilled across Canada. The Canadian Dental Care Plan (CDCP), which fully launched for all eligible populations earlier this year, will add to these staffing pressures on dental offices. [ORG] estimates that there will be a need for 2,300 additional dental assistants and 1,500 additional dental hygienists due to the CDCP alone.
